issue_title: Search also elsewhere in the system sys.path
issue_body:
When I tried checking out this tool, nothing seemed to work on my system. I tried gi, gtk, PyQt5 on Python 2.7 and 3.4, but I always got the typical ``ImportError: No module named gi``. After a lot of puzzling and digging around in the code, I found that vext is only searching in the system ``pip``'s install location (in my case ``/usr/``**``lib``**``/pythonX.Y/site-packages``).

So why is this a problem? Because for some reason, all Python packages from my distro's (Fedora 23) repos happen to be installed in ``/usr/``**``lib64``**``/pythonX.Y/site-packages``.

So I think vext should probably search everywhere in the system's ``sys.path`` that's not in the virtualenv's ``sys.path``, not only in ``pip``'s install location.

model = Model({y_1: x**2, y_2: x**3})

issue_title: Dotted keys
issue_body:
```
a.b.c = 1

# Equivalent to:
[a.b]
c = 1
```

This is a new feature added to what's going to become TOML 1.0:
https://github.com/toml-lang/toml/pull/505

Of course, TOML 1.0 is not released yet, and pytoml currently declares itself a TOML 0.4.0 parser. So it's not a bug that this doesn't work. But I hope that we can update pytoml once the new spec version is finalised. :-)

issue_title: Need padding for compatibility with RFC 5054
issue_body: The implementation here seems to be incompatible with the specification of SRP in RFC 5054 which requires padding in various places that is not applied here. For example, in [section 2.6](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5054#section-2.6) on computing the premaster secret, the server k value is defined as `k = SHA1(N | PAD(g))` where the `PAD` function means pad with zeros to match the byte length of `N`. The code here by contrast just computes `k = HASH(N | g)` with no padding. It seems like compatibility with TLS-SRP would be a nice feature to have here.

issue_title: Tutorials
issue_body:
I think one thing that is lacking in the current documentation is explicit description of the mapping between PyLaTeX classes and the resulting LaTeX output.

A natural mechanism for describing this mapping would be through tutorials. A tutorial could start out with a "target" tex file and break it down bit by bit, showing a part of the tex file and then describing how that output is achieved with PyLaTeX. A basic tutorial would go through things like sections, math, figures, etc. A more advanced tutorial could introduce custom commands/environments/containers. The latter would be especially helpful.

I wouldn't mind working on this, but I can't really discuss reasoning behind design decisions (which is another thing tutorials facilitate nicely). That can always be added later, of course.

issue_title: XDG.Preference('Gnome') decorator has no effect
issue_body:
The `XDG.Preference(name)` decorator is supposed to multiply priority by 1.5 if `$XDG_CURRENT_DESKTOP` matches the name.

GNOME and Secret Service backends call it with `name = 'Gnome'`.

However, that is never the case. On the mainline GNOME desktop the value is `GNOME` (uppercase), however on desktops derived from GNOME the following values of `$XDG_CURRENT_DESKTOP` are also possible:

* `GNOME-Classic:GNOME` (the semi-official session provided by gnome-shell-extensions package)
* `GNOME-Flashback:GNOME` (the GNOME Flashback session)
* `GNOME-Flashback:Unity` (the GNOME Flashback session on Ubuntu systems)
* `Unity` (Ubuntu's default desktop)
* `X-CINNAMON` (Mint's default desktop)
* …

Even more, the desktops that are not derived from GNOME and don't have their own keyring implementation (i.e. Xfce, LXDE and almost any non-KDE desktop) are usually also using the GNOME Keyring. For example, it is used in Lubuntu (the LXDE Ubuntu flavor).

So we should either update the check to make sure it catches all these variants, or drop it altogether (as it was doing nothing in its current form anyway). I prefer the latter variant.

issue_title: package cannot be built in non utf-8 environments
issue_body:
On the Ubuntu build daemons, $LC_ALL is C which causes Python 3's sys.getfilesystemencoding() to return 'ascii' instead of 'utf-8'.  This breaks setup.py because of the non-ascii characters in CHANGES.txt, so you get a dreaded UnicodeDecodeError:


```
% LC_ALL=C python3 setup.py --help
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "setup.py", line 23, in <module>
    long_description = open('README').read() + open('CHANGES.txt').read(),
  File "/usr/lib/python3.2/encodings/ascii.py", line 26, in decode
    return codecs.ascii_decode(input, self.errors)[0]
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xc3 in position 282: ordinal not in range(128)
```

issue_title: find_prompt with high latency connection with command authorization
issue_body:
Not sure if this is a known issue, but it seems find_prompt is unreliable when talking to device via a high latency/jitter connection with AAA command authorization enabled. Things get slightly better by increasing the delay_factor, but it seems this is still somewhat unreliable.

The gist of the issue seems to be that the find_prompt function sends X number of newlines, gets the last line as the prompt, then waits a moment and clears the buffer - however if there's delay (for example for AAA command authorization), the "moment" might not be enough and additional prompts arrive into the buffer after it has been cleared, which then of course means the send_command function sees an unexpected prompt and things get completely out of sync.

I modified a local copy of the find_prompt function so that it counts the number of prompts it has received and compares this to the number of newlines that were sent before the prompt was found - this seems to work with the (Cisco) devices I've been testing with, but wondering if there's a better way to do this. I'd be happy to post the changes I made here if that would be useful.

thanks!

issue_title: Add tests for OVS
C Extension not building on Windows for cp27/cp33/cp34
issue_body:
C Extension is not building on Windows for cp27/cp33/cp34.
This results in no speed-ups compared to the stock Python implementation. 

Root cause:
libbase64 requires a C99 compiler.
Windows CPython 2.7, 3.3 and 3.4 requires to build with Visual C++ 9.0 or Visual C++ 10.0 that are not compliant.
Refer to https://wiki.python.org/moin/WindowsCompilers.

Possible solutions:
1. Propose PR to upstream libbase64 to have support for those compilers. => unlikely to be merged.
2. Fork libbase64 to have support for those compilers. => I'd rather not.
3. Do nothing about it.

issue_title: Getting characteristics for empty services throws exception
issue_body:
bluepy seems to be unable to cope with peripherals which have empty services (i.e. they have no characteristics).

If you get services characteristics by calling the 'getCharacteristics()' method on an instance of a 'Service' object and that service has no characteristics, the exception "bluepy.btle.BTLEException: Error from Bluetooth stack (comerr)" is thrown.

"What is the point of services with no characteristics?", you may well ask but it seems that the BLE specification mandates the Generic Attribute service (0x1801) which includes only a single characteristic which is *optional*. So, it would seem that peripherals that have empty services might actually be quite common. Indeed I experienced this issue while trying to connect to Google's Daydream VR controller.

issue_title: CLI for generating completions
issue_body:
I wrote a simple CLI for generating completions (because I wanted to call jedi from outside of python), so I thought I’d ask whether you’d accept a contribution of something like it to `jedi/__main__.py`.

<details>

* I used `plistlib` for the output format because it fit my use case, I expect having to change that to maintain portability.
* I’m aware that the `__main__` block needs error handling.

~~~python
#!/usr/bin/env python

"""Usage: jedi <column> <line> [<path>]

Read Python source on stdin, parse it with Jedi, return its completions.
"""


from __future__ import print_function

import plistlib
import sys

import jedi


def main(line, column, path=None):
    source = sys.stdin.read()
    script = jedi.Script(source, line, column, path)
    return [{'display': c.name, 'image': c.type}
            for c in script.completions()]


def write(completions):
    print(plistlib.writePlistToString(completions))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    line, column, path = sys.argv[1:]
    write(main(int(line), int(column), path))
~~~

issue_title: Error when creating empty records with `make_calldata_tuple` and `_Call`
issue_body:
Hello PyVCF developers,

Some time ago I needed to write a VCF merging script that involves creating empty sample calls (for when a call at a given position is present in one sample but not the other). This involves the use of `vcf.model.make_calldata_tuple`.

The way I created the empty record was to simply get the expected `FORMAT` keys and use them like this:

```
empty_call_func = make_calldata_tuple(format_keys)
empty_call = _Call(None, sample_name, empty_call_func(*[None for n in format_keys]))
```

This worked when I was doing it on PyVCF 0.6.7. However in the most recent release (0.6.8), I stumbled on this error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/exports/test/vcf_merge.py", line 51, in <module>
    empty_call = _Call(None, sample, empty_cd_func(*[None for n in writer.template.formats]))
  File "/usr/local/sasc/miniconda3/envs/test/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vcf/model.py", line 27, in __init__
    self.gt_alleles = [(al if al != '.' else None) for al in allele_delimiter.split(self.data.GT)]
TypeError: expected string or bytes-like object
```

Upon checking it further, it seems the culprit was [this particular line](https://github.com/jamescasbon/PyVCF/blob/master/vcf/model.py#L26). It's not very apparent, but the check there is only for whether the attribute (which may well be `None`) is present or not. I think a more thorough check should be:

```
if hasattr(self.data, 'GT') and self.data.GT is not None:
```

There is also an additional error that assumes implicit conversion of `True` to an  `int` [here](https://github.com/jamescasbon/PyVCF/blob/master/vcf/model.py#L282). There, it seems that the better call should be:

```
return sum(1 for s in self.samples if s.called)
```

So that even when `s.called` is None, it will still work.

Now, I'm not exactly sure first if the way I created the empty record is the best (I used `_Call`, which I know is supposed to be private). So I'm open to suggestions of doing another way :). Still, I do think the previous two lines can be improved so that they're more error-resistant.

(by the way, these are only two simple code change so I thought I'd just open an issue here. Let me know if you prefer otherwise).

Thanks in advance for looking into this :).

issue_title: Supporting Lambda by using multiprocessing.Pipe
issue_body:
I'm working on a Lambda-based SSLyze pipeline, and one thing I came across is that Lambda doesn't support `multiprocess.Queue` or multiprocess.Pool`:

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/parallel-processing-in-python-with-aws-lambda/

But it does support `multiprocess.Pip`. The [multiprocessing docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/multiprocessing.html) say:

> The Pipe() function returns a pair of connection objects connected by a pipe which by default is duplex (two-way). For example:

```python
from multiprocessing import Process, Pipe

def f(conn):
    conn.send([42, None, 'hello'])
    conn.close()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    parent_conn, child_conn = Pipe()
    p = Process(target=f, args=(child_conn,))
    p.start()
    print(parent_conn.recv())   # prints "[42, None, 'hello']"
    p.join()
```
> The two connection objects returned by Pipe() represent the two ends of the pipe. Each connection object has send() and recv() methods (among others). Note that data in a pipe may become corrupted if two processes (or threads) try to read from or write to the same end of the pipe at the same time. Of course there is no risk of corruption from processes using different ends of the pipe at the same time.

Would it be tricky or dangerous to use `multiprocessing.Pipe` inside of SSLyze's concurrent scanning system, instead of `multiprocessing.Queue`? Are there any considerations or constraints it would impose on users?

I am not sure whether this would happen for every one, but at least I am not alone (e.g. http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/185529/why-does-querying-xrandr-briefly-freeze-the-screen).

It's not a bug for py3status but I think it would be great to mention it in the documentation (it takes me quite a while to locate the source of the problem). My solution is to set the `cache_timeout` of `xrandr` module to a very large number, and it would still update it's value if I click it.

TtHFanfic.org Fetch URLs not retrieving all stories from page
issue_body:
TtH by default restricts story rating to FR18.

if is_adult is false, then the current behavior is IMO correct.

In this case, I have set is_adult to true, and also have provided login credentials. My TtH account's default rating is further set to FR21.

Download works fine for stories rated FR21 when credentials are provided, but when fetching URLs from a page, it appears that the user is not logged in and the default story rating restriction of FR18 applies, therefore not all stories are downloaded.

is_adult is set to true globally in this case; if it was false I would expect the current behavior.

Example:
For https://www.tthfanfic.org/AuthorStories-20566/Katrina.htm
Expected output (all ratings):
```
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29997-9/Katrina+BTVS+Builders+Junkyard+Dog.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29830-4/Katrina+BTVS+Builders.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-28886/Katrina+Art+and+Banners+and+Such.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-27523/Katrina+BTVS+AB+It+s+not+a+case+of+But.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-26962/Katrina+TDWP+BTVS+On+a+Wing+and+a+Prayer.htm
```
Actual output (only FR18 and below):
```
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29997-9/Katrina+BTVS+Builders+Junkyard+Dog.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29830-4/Katrina+BTVS+Builders.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-27523/Katrina+BTVS+AB+It+s+not+a+case+of+But.htm
```

Note that there is a special case in the code for AO3 to log in because AO3 does not show adult stories without it; that is not exactly the case for TtH, but I've described my findings below.

There does seem to be a session cookie, so if a session can be opened for fetching URLs, then fetching this URL should set the max rating to FR21:
https://www.tthfanfic.org/setmaxrating.php?sitemaxrating=5
After that point, the original page could be fetched.

There is not a specific cookie that saves the default rating.

The other option, of course, is simply to log in prior to fetching, as is done for AO3, in which case the account's specified default maximum rating would apply.

issue_title: Investigate "full" set of tests
issue_body:
During our discussions, we frequently came to the conclusion that we wouldn't test some things, as they are implicitly tested by other test cases or unlikely to fail. This includes things like

- Testing both the global functions and the `SoundFile` methods.
- Extending tests to run every combination of stereo/mono and int/float test data
- Running tests for more file formats (wav, flac, ogg, etc.).

Implementing these tests would multiply the number of tests, while not necesserily resulting in a much higher coverage of the Python code. It might however catch some combination of preconditions that our current tests don't catch.

Since these tests would take more time to run than our existing tests, it would probably be a good idea to not run them by default (not sure how to do that yet).

It should change to something like this:

  cdef void* temp_buffer

# Title
explanation

example code

issue_title: Provide fallback database implementation when sqlite is not available
issue_body:
Some platforms ship the sqlite library separately from core Python (bsd) or don't implement it at all (Jython). This is annoying, because it means Hypothesis doesn't work because it tries to import sqlite3.

One option would just be to disable the database feature in this case, but that seems a shame. It would be preferable to use a fallback option, or possibly to switch to a non sqlite3 based implementation altogether.

Two possible options:

1. Write a backend that uses the anydbm module, which has a good fallback chain including a (slow) pure Python implementation that works anywhere.
2. Write a backend that just uses basic file system operations and e.g. stores a directory per key.

I do not have a strong preference between the two, but the fact that you would have to serialize lists to strings somehow for 1 and this gives you complex append behaviour seems fragile. On the other hand so does writing what is basically a database.

This may require some attention to the database API.

Note: This is marked as help-wanted, but it's a particularly ambitious help-wanted which will probably take a lot of work.

issue_title: Feature request: Add option to avoid reading chart cache data
issue_body:
By default, and unseen to the user, the chart.py module reads cell data from the worksheets that charts series refer to and stores it as cached data values in the chart.

This isn't strictly required by Excel but it increases compatibility and ensures that charts are rendered correctly by third part apps and in particular by PowerPoint.

However, for certain complex ranges the data reference can't (easily) be parsed. The currently workaround is to specify populated or empty data lists:

```python
chart.add_series({'categories':      '=chart.xlsx!Categories1', 
                  'values':          '=chart.xlsx!Series2',
                  'categories_data': [], 
                  'values_data':     []})
````
A cleaner solution may be to add an option to explicitly not try to cache data:
```python
chart.add_series({'categories': '=chart.xlsx!Categories1', 
                  'values':     '=chart.xlsx!Series2',
                  'cached_data': False})
````

It could also be fixed with some try/except code to catch various parse issues.

issue_title: Without `--wait`, shell subprocesses can deadlock / are not reaped
issue_body:
For example:

```
watchmedo shell-command -c 'cat' /some/file
```

The relevant code:

```
        self.process = subprocess.Popen(command, shell=True)                                    
        if self.wait_for_process:                                                               
            self.process.wait()    
```

One thing to note, the subprocess will inherit watchmedo's file descriptors, which maybe isn't desirable. (Or maybe it is?) In this case it means `cat` will never exit since stdin is inherited from `watchmedo`, which if `watchmedo` was run from an interactive shell (for example) is a TTY that may never be closed. So an endless stream of cats which never exit will be spawned.

But also, `wait(2)` is never called, so even if the subprocess does exit, it's never reaped. This accumulates zombie processes until `watchmedo` exits, at which point `init` will inherit those subprocesses and reap them. Of course that may never happen. You can see this with `ps -f` for example:

```
29547 ?        Sl     3:00  \_ /usr/bin/python /usr/local/bin/watchmedo shell-command -R -c [...] --noinput [...]
19616 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
19680 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
19682 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
19724 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
19735 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
19896 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
20063 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
20530 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
23257 ?        Z      0:00      \_ [sh] <defunct>
```

One solution would be making `--wait` the default -- it's what I'd want personally for the use cases I have in mind.

Alternately, something needs to wait around for the child processes to exit. That could be a thread which calls `wait` which will block, or something like `epoll_wait` can do it in a non-blocking way.

issue_title: Changing default Python version from 2 to 3 breaks installation on Fedora
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm using the repository version of trash-cli on a Fedora 25 workstation. Recently, I changed my default Python version from 2 to 3. Since then, running e. g. trash-put causes an error:

~~~
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/trash-put", line 4, in <module>
    from trashcli.put import main as main
ImportError: No module named 'trashcli'
~~~

The reason seems to be that the python modules used by trashcli are installed into the python2 directories:

~~~
dnf repoquery -l trash-cli
Last metadata expiration check: 17:32:05 ago on Tue Jun 27 15:35:09 2017.
/usr/bin/trash
/usr/bin/trash-empty
/usr/bin/trash-list
/usr/bin/trash-put
/usr/bin/trash-restore
/usr/bin/trash-rm
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trash_cli-0.12.10.3_-py2.7.egg-info
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/__init__.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/__init__.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/__init__.pyo
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/cmds.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/cmds.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/cmds.pyo
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/fs.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/fs.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/fs.pyo
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/fstab.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/fstab.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/fstab.pyo
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/list_mount_points.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/list_mount_points.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/list_mount_points.pyo
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/put.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/put.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/put.pyo
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/rm.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/rm.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/rm.pyo
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/trash.py
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/trash.pyc
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/trashcli/trash.pyo
/usr/share/doc/trash-cli
/usr/share/doc/trash-cli/README.rst
/usr/share/man/man1/trash-empty.1.gz
/usr/share/man/man1/trash-list.1.gz
/usr/share/man/man1/trash-put.1.gz
/usr/share/man/man1/trash-restore.1.gz
/usr/share/man/man1/trash-rm.1.gz
~~~

A way to fix this would be to explicitly ask for the python2/3 interpreter in the shebang line of the top-level scripts `usr/bin/trash*`, i.e change the first line to `#!/usr/bin/python2` or `#!/usr/bin/python3`
 depending on the python version for which the backend module was installed. Shouldn't this be part of the regular setup / package installation routine?

issue_title: EOL for py26 and py33 support
issue_body:
[CHANGES.rst](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/blob/master/CHANGES.rst) for the 2.1.0 release announced EOL for py26 and py33 support, as both have been EOL for a while and now pytest has dropped support for them.

Cut a 3.0.0 release that removes py26 and py33 support and testing. Be sure to clearly communicate this and update:

* [ ] setup.py classifiers
* [ ] tox.ini including unpinning any versions pinned for compatibility with those

b = 255
a is b
True

a = 257
b = 257
a is b
False

issue_title: CoxPHFitter.predict() gives many 'inf' values
issue_body:
The Cox proportional hazards method "predict" returns 'inf' the majority of the time, on average, for several data sets. For example:

```
from lifelines.datasets import load_rossi
from lifelines import CoxPHFitter
rossi_dataset = load_rossi()
df_train = rossi_dataset.head(300)
cf = CoxPHFitter()
cf.fit(df_train, 'week', 'arrest')
df_test = rossi_dataset.tail(100)
df_test['pred'] = cf.predict(df_test)
df_test['pred'].describe()
count    100.000000
mean            inf
std             NaN
min       50.000000
25%             inf
50%             inf
75%             inf
max             inf
Name: pred, dtype: float64
```

On several other data sets I also get 'inf' values at least at the 50% level, which seems to suggest numerical instability. 

A related stability/accumulation point issue is also seen with the Aalen additive fitter returning repeated values via its predict() method. In general, I get a single repeated predicted value on over 80% of entries in test sets with num_rows between 1k and 10k.
**Question: could someone compare lifelines results with results from R or SAS?**

issue_title: python-memcached should fail gracefully when the server does not exist.
issue_body:
I recently ran into a situation where our build server did not have a running instance of memcached.  I would have expected things to fail gracefully.  (Clearly, there wouldn't be any caching happening.  Each request would simply have to re-fetch any cached data.)  But instead of failing gracefully, the caching code failed after the connect attempt to the memcached server failed.

It looks like once the server is marked dead there are some additional calls to cmemcache_hash that get made with strings as keys.  To reproduce this, find a server without a memcached instance and try this:

`python
Python 3.5.1 (default, Apr  6 2016, 16:48:42) 
[GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-11)] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import memcache
>>> cache = memcache.Client(['127.0.0.1:11211'], debug=1)
>>> cache.set("working?",1,1)
MemCached: MemCache: inet:127.0.0.1:11211: connect: [Errno 111] Connection refused.  Marking dead.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/<snipped>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memcache.py", line 700, in set
    return self._set("set", key, val, time, min_compress_len, noreply)
  File "/<snipped>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memcache.py", line 983, in _set
    server, key = self._get_server(key)
  File "/<snipped>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memcache.py", line 413, in _get_server
    serverhash = serverHashFunction(str(serverhash) + str(i))
  File "/<snipped>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memcache.py", line 65, in cmemcache_hash
    (((binascii.crc32(key) & 0xffffffff)
**TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'str'**`

So, I temporarily "fixed" the issue with something like this:

`--- /<snipped>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memcache.py	2016-04-07 17:23:41.428028602 -0500
+++ /<snipped>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memcache.py_fixed	2016-04-07 17:23:13.606003863 -0500
@@ -61,6 +61,9 @@
 
 
 def cmemcache_hash(key):
+    if isinstance(key, six.text_type):
+        key = key.encode('utf-8')
+
     return (
         (((binascii.crc32(key) & 0xffffffff)
           >> 16) & 0x7fff) or 1)
`

I'm not sure if this is the right approach to correct the issue or not.  Hopefully we can get back to a point where python-memcached can fail gracefully when the underlying service is absent.

Thanks,

Matthew Lee

issue_title: Getting unintended echo responses when sending messages too fast.
issue_body:
I was getting echo responses from the server even though I have not subscribed to them. After digging into it I found that in [wsgi_server.py:106](https://github.com/jrief/django-websocket-redis/blob/master/ws4redis/wsgi_server.py#L106) you define `recvmsg = None` and then later use the value of that ([on line 119](https://github.com/jrief/django-websocket-redis/blob/master/ws4redis/wsgi_server.py#L119)) to determine if you are sending the same message that just came in. I am finding this breaks down fairly quickly when sending message even at a rate of 5 to 10 fps.

A quick solution I came up with to solve this is to use a deque to track the last 10 messages rather than just the last single message.  I foun this is easily keeping up even at a rate of 200 fps. The code change is pretty simple..

    from collections import deque

    # save most recent 10 items (rather than single most recent)
    recent = deque(maxlen=10)
    
    # when a message comes in..
    recent.append(recvmsg)

    # change the check on line 119 to..
    if sendmsg and (echo_message or sendmsg != recvmsg):

issue_title: Regular expression in patch_xml problem - loosing tags
issue_body:
I have a DOCX template like this:
```
Available answers:
    {% for answer in answers %}{{r answer.text }}
    {% endfor %}
```
which produces a XML snippet 
```
<w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Normal"/><w:rPr><w:b w:val="false"/><w:b w:val="false"/><w:bCs w:val="false"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r><w:rPr><w:b w:val="false"/><w:bCs w:val="false"/><w:sz w:val="16"/><w:szCs w:val="16"/></w:rPr><w:t>Available answers:</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Normal"/><w:ind w:left="709" w:hanging="0"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Verdana" w:hAnsi="Verdana"/><w:sz w:val="16"/><w:szCs w:val="16"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r><w:rPr><w:b w:val="false"/><w:bCs w:val="false"/><w:sz w:val="16"/><w:szCs w:val="16"/></w:rPr><w:t>{% for answer in answers %}{{r answer.text }}</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Normal"/><w:ind w:left="709" w:hanging="0"/><w:rPr/></w:pPr><w:r><w:rPr><w:b w:val="false"/><w:bCs w:val="false"/><w:sz w:val="16"/><w:szCs w:val="16"/></w:rPr><w:t>{% endfor %}</w:t></w:r></w:p>
```
And the regular expression in patch_xml causing problems:
```
<w:r[ >](?:(?!<w:r[ >]).)*({%|{{)r ([^}%]*(?:%}|}})).*?</w:r>
```
When this code is passed through patch_xml method, the {% for %} tag is accidentally removed and rendering fails on  Encountered unknown tag 'endfor' message
Output:
```
<w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Normal"/><w:rPr><w:b w:val="false"/><w:b w:val="false"/><w:bCs w:val="false"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r><w:rPr><w:b w:val="false"/><w:bCs w:val="false"/><w:sz w:val="16"/><w:szCs w:val="16"/></w:rPr><w:t>Available answers:</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Normal"/><w:ind w:left="709" w:hanging="0"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Verdana" w:hAnsi="Verdana"/><w:sz w:val="16"/><w:szCs w:val="16"/></w:rPr></w:pPr>{{ answer.text }}</w:p><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Normal"/><w:ind w:left="709" w:hanging="0"/><w:rPr/></w:pPr><w:r><w:rPr><w:b w:val="false"/><w:bCs w:val="false"/><w:sz w:val="16"/><w:szCs w:val="16"/></w:rPr><w:t>{% endfor %}</w:t></w:r></w:p>
```

The correct behaviour would be to leave all Jinja markup in the output:
```{% for answer in answers %}{{r answer.text }}```

Regex101 snippet: https://regex101.com/r/3I5Otf/2

issue_title: Initialize asyncio reactor with preconfigured ThreadPoolExecutor to control thread counts
issue_body:
Daphne currently initializes an asyncio reactor with no default executor, so when something like aioredis is used (as it is in redis_channels), a default ThreadPoolExecutor is created on first execute call which has cpu_count * 5 threads as default (which is suboptimal for many deployments).

Consider adding something like the following to daphne's server.py where plain asyncioreactor.install() is being used now:

from asyncio.events import new_event_loop
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

    event_loop = new_event_loop()
    threadpool = ThreadPoolExecutor(
        max_workers=(
            int(os.environ["ASIO_THREADS"])
            if "ASIO_THREADS" in os.environ
            else None
        )
    )
    event_loop.set_default_executor(threadpool)
    asyncioreactor.install(event_loop)

When testing on OSX, I was able to control the asyncioreactor thread pool size using the ASIO_THREADS env variable with this code.

issue_title: Generalized batch_dot / matrix multiplication
issue_body:
Currently, mx.{nd,sym}.batch_dot is constrained to 3d input where the first dimension is considered the batch size.
It would be great to be able to have n-dimensional inputs to a matrix multiplication and do the matrix multiplication on the last two dimensions only. This avoids having to combine/split dimensions before and after.
Desired behavior:
```
# i1: (d1, ... dn, m, x)
# i2: (d1, ... dn, x, n)
out = mx.sym.batch_dot(lhs=i1, rhs=i2)
# out: (d1, ... dn, m, n)
```
The `transpose_*` flags would always only transpose the last two dimensions.

issue_title: The `then` method on the `Deferred` object is executed asynchronously
Views should show the next upcoming recurrence of event
issue_body:
Currently all kind of standard views show the first (main) date of an event. If you use a collection to show all upcoming events then events show up who's first event has passed but has an upcoming recurrence.

The problem is that although the collection selects the upcoming recurrence of the event, it will still show the first (already passed) event in the list. This confuses users as they don't even click on it because the date that is shown is in the past and so they think this event isn't active anymore although it has an upcoming recurrence.

issue_title: When Key retriever fails to get the key on HTTP Error, TypeError exception is propagated instead of ValueError
issue_body:
<!--- When key retriever fails to get the key on HTTP Error, TypeError exception is propagated instead of ValueError -->

## Expected Behavior
When key retriever fails to get the key on HTTP Error, for example a 403 Forbidden HTTPError, TypeError exception is propagated instead of ValueError

## Current Behavior
When a *KeyRetrieveException is raised, the wrapped exception mechanism used in exception.py **borked** on a TypeError exception due to args tuple assignment: 

```
 exception stack: Traceback (most recent call last):
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/atlassian_jwt_auth/key.py", line 97, in retrieve
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    return self._retrieve(url, requests_kwargs)
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/atlassian_jwt_auth/key.py", line 108, in _retrieve
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    resp.raise_for_status()
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/requests/models.py", line 935, in raise_for_status
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    raise HTTPError(http_error_msg, response=self)
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	requests.exceptions.HTTPError: 403 Client Error: Forbidden for url: https://asap-distribution--app.ap-southeast-2.dev.paas-inf.net/test/a9e1e36e-2d29-4530-9ba8-98a68904cb89
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	Traceback (most recent call last):
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/src/app/src/lib/asap.py", line 81, in decorated
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    asap_claims = parse_jwt(auth.split(" ")[1])
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/src/app/src/lib/asap.py", line 64, in parse_jwt
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    verify=config.asap_cfg['verify_https_cert'])
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/atlassian_jwt_auth/verifier.py", line 30, in verify_jwt
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    public_key = self._retrieve_pub_key(key_identifier, requests_kwargs)
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/atlassian_jwt_auth/verifier.py", line 38, in _retrieve_pub_key
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    key_identifier, **requests_kwargs)
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/atlassian_jwt_auth/key.py", line 103, in retrieve
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    raise PublicKeyRetrieverException(e, status_code=status_code)
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/atlassian_jwt_auth/exceptions.py", line 27, in __init__
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    super(_WithStatus, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/atlassian_jwt_auth/exceptions.py", line 13, in __init__
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	    args[0] = str(orig)
error	24-Nov-2017 00:14:36	TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment
```


## Possible Solution
Avoid the tuple assignment and populate the args in a temporary list, see https://github.com/atlassian/asap-authentication-python/pull/69 for details

```
class _WrappedException(object):
    """Allow wrapping exceptions in a new class while preserving the original
    as an attribute.

    Note that while Python 2 and 3 both have reasonable ways to handle this,
    they're mutually incompatible. This is a simple, portable approach that
    should be sufficient for most use cases.
    """
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        wrapped_args = [arg for arg in args]

        if args:
            orig = args[0]
            if isinstance(orig, Exception):

                wrapped_args[0] = str(orig)
                self.original_exception = getattr(orig, 'original_exception',
                                                  orig)
        super(_WrappedException, self).__init__(*wrapped_args, **kwargs)
```

## Steps to Reproduce

You can add the following test in test_public_key_provider.py
```
    @mock.patch.object(requests.Session, 'get',
                       side_effect=requests.exceptions.HTTPError(mock.Mock(response=mock.Mock(status_code=403)),
                                                                 'forbidden'))
    def test_retrieve_fails_with_forbidden_error(self, mock_get_method):
        """ tests that the retrieve method fails when the response is an
        403 forbidden error.
        """
        _setup_mock_response_for_retriever(
            mock_get_method, self._public_key_pem)
        retriever = self.create_retriever(self.base_url)
        with self.assertRaises(ValueError):
            retriever.retrieve('example/eg')
```

issue_title: Luigi workers should report dead workers to scheduler
issue_body:
This is a frequent annoyance:
INFO - 2014-09-11 16:02:49,449 - JobThing(date=2014-09-10, build=2014-W32) is currently run by worker Worker(salt=178277472, host=edgenode-a1.spotify.net, username=as, pid=22199)

If the hostname of the worker doing the scheduler check is the same host reported as the worker currently running the job, the least it could do is see if the pid is still alive and if not, report it dead to the scheduler and rerun the task.

issue_title: what we talk about when we talk about _files
issue_body:
The contents of the `_files` field for Literature record is supposed to contain the metadata to retrieve the file by `invenio-records-files`.

The schema we have for it was copied by Zenodo and so contains the basic info in the [invenio-records-files schema](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/blob/master/invenio_records_files/jsonschemas/records-files/records-files-v1.0.0.json), but also some additional zenodo-specific stuff (`previewer`, `type`) that we probably don't need.

The workflow is using this field in yet another way, writing `description` and `doctype` there (for arXiv PDF and extracted plots), which are not currently in the schema. This doesn't cause any error now as the results of `_files` are discarded anyway and never sent to legacy, but we should decide on what information we really want to have there. 

@kaplun and @tsgit know how files ares handled on legacy and could share their experience.
Discussing with @jacquerie, we identified the following keys that might be useful:

* `doctype` (or `document_type`?): to signal what kind of document is attached. This would be an `enum` with values `fulltext`, `plot`, **what else**?
* `mime_type`: how this document is encoded, which might warrant a different handling (e.g. PDF vs XML for a fulltext).
* `hidden`: a flag to indicate whether this file is publicly visible (would be true for fulltexts used for indexing that we may not serve directly to our users).

issue_title: Please provide a way to install pipenv without using vendored dependencies
issue_body:
As discussed in #1885 it is very hard for downstream (i.e. Debian) maintainers to package `pipenv` when it provides almost all its dependencies in the `vendor` and `patched` directory.

Quoting myself from https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1885#issuecomment-377778859

> My main concern is still security: If your dependencies also handle their dependencies by vendoring them (and theirs) you'll end up with multiple versions of the same package inside your vendors directory (e.g. the requests package). If one of those packages needs an update you'll have a hard time fixing all of them. For downstream maintainers (Debian, Suse, etc) it is even worse as we usually just update the problematic library and assume this bug to be fixed for all packages depending on it. Now we actively have to search for copies of that library everywhere.

So in order to make life for downstream package maintainers easier, please provide a way -- similar to what `pip` does -- to install `pipenv` "unbundeled" and thus using the packages installed on the target system.

issue_title: Stop patching write_pkg_info?
issue_body: According to [this comment](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/89ef5bb7e4812193d6f593d531bad36191517981/setuptools/monkey.py#L114), `write_pkg_info` is only patched because of a bug in 3.2.2. Now that setuptools has dropped support for Python 3.2, maybe that patch is no longer necessary?

issue_title: update docs to fit PEP470
issue_body:
pypi, pip and easy_install all seem to not support the use of `download_url` for scraping external repositories since PEP470 (not even through pip's `--allow-external` option). The documentation should probably reflect that, especially in the ["Making Your Package Available for EasyInstall"](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#making-your-package-available-for-easyinstall) section.

Additionally, the mentions of running `setup.py register` should probably also be removed, since this has been a noop and unsupported for a while.

issue_title: Remove_object change?
issue_body:
Hi,

I am working on my minio storage backend adapter for django (https://github.com/tomhoule/django-minio-storage), and I noticed that a test case I wrote a few weeks ago started failing with the latest version of minio-py.

Minio-py used to throw a `ResponseError` when trying to delete a non-existent object, and now it doesn't. I couldn't find information about this here so I'm reporting it in case it was not intentional. Feel free to close the issue if it was.

Sorry I can't be more precise with version numbers, I am just starting to put the project under CI.

issue_title: Increasingly high CPU utilization in 0.7.0
issue_body:
## Overview

After upgrading `dateparser` from `0.6.0` to `0.7.0` a program that normally had very low CPU usage began taking up an entire CPU.    Any time it was restarted, the CPU usage would trend upwards until it was around 100%. 

The was encountered with Python 2 (`2.7.9` and `2.7.14`).  We have seen this happening on Debian, CentOS, and MacOS.   In each case, pinning the version to `0.6.0` resolves the issue.

 ## Test Case

The following code can be used to cause the issue:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python2                                                                                                  

import dateparser
import time

cycle = 1

while True:
    start_time = time.time()

    for i in range(0, 50):
        dateparser.parse('Sun Apr  1 01:00:53 EDT 2018')
        dateparser.parse('invalid')

    elapsed_time = time.time() - start_time

    print "Round %d: %.2f seconds" % (cycle, elapsed_time)

    cycle += 1
    time.sleep(1)
```

We encounter the high CPU utilization only when we attempt to parse both valid and invalid dates.   

## Example Runs

### 0.6.0

When run with version `0.6.0`, the amount of time needed to parse the dates each cycle remains flat:

```
Round 1: 13.24 seconds
Round 2: 15.53 seconds
Round 3: 13.46 seconds
Round 4: 13.32 seconds
Round 5: 13.43 seconds
Round 6: 13.22 seconds

issue_title: Put Changes.rst updates in tag
issue_body:
Hiya!  This is just a pedantic request!  I've noticed that in the last two tags, the update to the CHANGES.rst for the changes in the tag arrived in a commit to master after the tag was made.  This confused me for 2.1.0, as I was only looking at the CHANGES.rst file for that tag, and wasn't sure what had made it in.

In the future, could you modify the CHANGES.rst file before that tag is made?  In my own projects, I've done this by updating the changelog in the commit that actually made a change.  E.g., you could maintain a 2.1.1 (SNAPSHOT) (or (ALPHA) or (WHATEVER)) tag at the top of CHANGES.rst, and then change it so it says the date of the tag when you make the tag.

Or do whatever you want! :)  Thank you!

issue_title: Add pykafka recipe to conda-forge
issue_body:
It might be useful to have a `pykafka` recipe on conda-forge.  This would automatically build conda packages for various platforms and would probably help users looking to use pykafka with librdkafka, for which there is already a [binary package](https://github.com/conda-forge/librdkafka-feedstock/).  

[Adding a recipe](https://conda-forge.org/#add_recipe) is relatively easy.  I'm happy to help, though the conda-forge maintainers are also some of the most helpful devs that I've met online.  

There might also be some opportunity to help pykafka automatically find librdkafka when installed in this manner.

issue_title: TPRegion should output 2 new fields: active & predicted columns separately
issue_body:
Depends on https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1450

It will be useful if the output of the TP region provided separate fields for the `active` & `predicted` columns; 

currently these are combined to the default output as defined by the TP outputType - either of them, or both. 

Use case: AnomalyRegion could depend only on TPRegion (and not SP, which is now required to get the active columns, [see](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/examples/network/network_api_demo.py#L158) ), I wonder if the [lrnActiveCells](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/regions/TPRegion.py#L576) is the same as activeOut suggested for this usecase (maybe not, as it's only used in `learn` phase)

TODO:
- [ ] TPRegion.output provides new activeOut and predictedOut fields
- [ ] also we should remove [anomalyScore](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/regions/TPRegion.py#L568) from TPRegion, SPRegion output, and use AnomalyRegion only

issue_title: Set up CMake with add_subdirectory and ExternalProject
issue_body:
- top level CMakeLists.txt that delegates externals to `external/CMakeLists.txt` with `add_subdirectory` and nupic_core to `src/CMakeLists.txt`
- externals will use ExternalProject if they can't be found locally (users can set `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH` if deps are installed in non-standard locations)
- nupic_core will simply specify which libraries to link against and assume `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH`, etc are set correctly

issue_title: confusion between variance vs. std dev of noise
issue_body:
I found a bug in UncorrelatedNoise or perhaps something related.  If you do this:
```
import galsim
im = galsim.Image(256,256)
ucn = galsim.UncorrelatedNoise(variance=0.1)
im.addNoise(ucn)
print im.array.std(), im.array.var()
```
then you'll get a standard deviation of 0.1, variance of 0.01.  But I had specified variance=0.1.  So something is getting labeled incorrectly somewhere in UncorrelatedNoise or the routines that it calls.

I will dig into this some more in a bit, just wanted to open the issue.

@emhuff, this isn't all of the problem you're seeing, but it's definitely part of it.

issue_title: clearly document that x->col and y->row
issue_body:
In C/C++ and numpy the order of indexing is
```
image[row, col]
```
which by convention is understood as "y, x".  I think this ordering is also followed by galsim Image objects since you can get a direct view as a numpy array.

A number of galsim functions (eg. shift()) take a tuple of arguments listed in the docs as (x,y) but it isn't clear if that is meant to follow the same ordering as images (row,col) or be equivalent to (col, row).  By word of mouth I have heard it is indeed (col,row), so the (row->y)  (col->x) relation holds, it is just the arguments are given in reverse.

Since this ordering is reverse of C/C++/python/galsim.Image conventions I think it is worth a big notice in the docs up front.  Sorry if this is clearly stated and I just missed it.

issue_title: Allergen specific class of icons
issue_body:
There was a [request in 2014 that was subsequently closed for an allergy specific class of icons](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/issues/5228). Since it's closed it doesn't come up in generic searches and has had only a little commentary, so **this is more of a request to re-open that issue/request** and merge new more specific allergy requests into that, or create a separate set of requests for each (but wouldn't a single set be better?)

![from #5228 ](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5088889/41752863-355ea28e-760d-11e8-97af-61a3c442fee7.png)
![from #8324 ](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/16594261/12288779/76632556-b9d7-11e5-887c-d2d7c2a5b7a5.png)

As mentioned in the message of #5228 (the original request to which I am referring, and in the comments) this is a requirement in the Netherlands, and France and I assume other EU countries. AFAIK it is not a requirement in my location in my classification of foods, but as a restaurateur it is something I want clearly visible on my media.

as noted in a comment to #8324 (a similarly closed allergens request) I realise this is a comprehensive set of icons, but like #1895 ([DO NOT +1] Food Icon Categories) which deals with specifically food related icons, allergens/allergy icons could be included within the [food (category)](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/labels/food%20%28category%29), and not necessarily created specifically as allergens.

My comments:
* Icons should follow a similar thought/design process to make them more uniform.
* an icon for includes as well as a does not include (could just be the crossed circle overlaid). 
* allergen and food contents could be grouped together

## closed (please reopen?)
#5228 Req: Allergens

## currently open (could be merged)
#2405 icon-sheaf, icon-sheaf-ban (Wheat / Gluten Free)
#8737 icon-sesame
#8736 icon-crustacean
#7493 icon nut (acorn)


*As with all issues, people shouldn't be adding a +1 comment, please find your favourite issues/requests and **add a like** indicated by a **thumbs up** underneath. If one isn't already present that means all the +1 under it are moot. Go to the top right of the post and click the emoji add button. That gives a nice count for everyone to see, especially the developers! :)*

*I couldn't find how to add labels, maybe only the devs can do this, but this would be related to the food category.*

issue_title: tox: Should the py26 testenv be kept ?
issue_body:
As a potential contributor, the first thing I do when starting to work on a project is run the test suite.

When I clone `tower-cli` and run `tox` I end up with:

```
docs: commands succeeded                                                  
flake8: commands succeeded                                                
ERROR:   py26: commands failed                                            
py27: commands succeeded                                                  
py34: commands succeeded                                                  
py35: commands succeeded                                                  
py36: commands succeeded   
```

They are 208 occurrences of the same failure: `ValueError: zero length field name in format`.
(ie. `prefix = urljoin(prefix, "{}/".format(CUR_API_VERSION))`)

It happens because the format expression has been used without indices. Expression that has become valid in py27+ and py32+.

Looking at `HISTORY.rst` I can see that in the notes of `3.1.8`: `Begin process to deprecate python 2.6.`

So should the py26 tox testenv be kept?

issue_title: Running patronictl list on a leader on a Consul cluster removes the leader's lock
issue_body:
We are using Consul as DCS and noticed that running `patronictl list -c ...yml clustername` on a cluster leader using its .yml will demote the leader dirtily, giving no warning and causing a failover.

The cause seems to be this:
https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/dcs/consul.py#L157

Patronictl will create a new session with the name specified on the .yml (the leader one), look for existing leader sessions, find one with matching name but different session ID (the one from the actual Patroni daemon) and decide to remove the lock.

It seems this behavior might make sense if it runs on the daemon itself, but definetly not on patronictl

issue_title: casting to str() in error handling causes UnicodeEncodeErrors
issue_body:
### Description

I have some fields in an application that need to be restricted to ascii. After implementing a format for that, I found that Connexion's error handling of the ValidationError that was raised throws an exception because the error string contains some unicode characters, and `str()` is used to try to make it printable. For example:
```
balrogadmin_1  | 2018-06-15 14:49:26,132 - ERROR - PID: 1537 - Request: 140481394377808 - flask.app.log_exception#1761: Exception on /users/ÁÁ/permissions/emergency_shutoff [PUT]
balrogadmin_1  | Traceback (most recent call last):
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 2292, in wsgi_app
balrogadmin_1  |     response = self.full_dispatch_request()
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1815, in full_dispatch_request
balrogadmin_1  |     rv = self.handle_user_exception(e)
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1718, in handle_user_exception
balrogadmin_1  |     reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1813, in full_dispatch_request
balrogadmin_1  |     rv = self.dispatch_request()
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1799, in dispatch_request
balrogadmin_1  |     return self.view_functions[rule.endpoint](**req.view_args)
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/connexion/decorators/decorator.py", line 73, in wrapper
balrogadmin_1  |     response = function(request)
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/connexion/decorators/validation.py", line 122, in wrapper
balrogadmin_1  |     response = function(request)
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/connexion/decorators/validation.py", line 279, in wrapper
balrogadmin_1  |     error = self.validate_path_parameter(param, request)
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/connexion/decorators/validation.py", line 241, in validate_path_parameter
balrogadmin_1  |     return self.validate_parameter('path', val, param)
balrogadmin_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/connexion/decorators/validation.py", line 208, in validate_parameter
balrogadmin_1  |     converted_value=converted_value.encode(),
balrogadmin_1  | UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 0-1: ordinal not in range(128)
```

Using `str()` in Python 2 uses the default encoding, which in Python 2 is almost always ascii - so we get this exception.


### Expected behaviour

No exception!

### Actual behaviour

UnicodeEncodeError

### Steps to reproduce

Raise a ValidationError that contains non-ascii characters in it.

### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

```

root@70a68612da53:/app# python --version
Python 2.7.15
root@70a68612da53:/app# pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"
Version: 1.4.2
```

issue_title: Error when using it with step-functions parallel state
issue_body:
**Context**: [Step functions](https://aws.amazon.com/step-functions/) is a technology aws offers to coordinate and manage the execution of your lambdas.

**Issue**: Lambda function can have as input parameters, an `event` and a `context`. 
`event` is always guaranteed to be a `dict`. 

In step-functions you can have multiple lambda functions get executed in [parallel](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/amazon-states-language-parallel-state.html). The generated output of this parallel state, is an array with one element for each branch containing the output from that branch. That means that a lambda function that will get called after  this parallel state, will have the parameter `event` being of type `list`  instead of a `dict`. 

So when you try to report to sentry using `raven-python-lambda` in this lambda function that `event` is not a dict, and so it breaks. It breaks because of the numerous `event.get` calls in the `__init__.py` file.  Error is `AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'get'`

I was thinking of creating a PR that simply will check if the type of `event` is a `dict` before doing attempting the `event.get`. 

Do you guys think this is acceptable? Any thoughts around that? Will be happy to chat about this more.

issue_title: Currently a way to pull Cognito Auth claims from the request?
issue_body:
I'm interested in logging information about which Cognito authorized users are making chalice requests. Briefly in late 2016/early 2017, it looks like the Cognito claims were included as a top level item on the Request object: https://github.com/aws/chalice/commit/b5030ca645c95db112f50e4fa9a06a4a5e9aaf32#diff-92c4bff78ea0a0fdf94c7dae50063163

This object was removed here:
https://github.com/aws/chalice/pull/240

with the comment, "Need to figure out claims key. This was originally a top level key,
and I believe this is now moved into the request context. Need to double check."

As far as I can tell, the claims aren't currently being passed along with my request context. I am getting the token in my authorization header, and in the short term I can decode that for the user information, but I'm not thrilled with that as a long term solution.

Is there currently a straightforward way to access Cognito claims that I am missing? Or does that need attention.

Thanks!

issue_title: Continuation of processing after response sent.
issue_body:
I've had a look at the documentation and code but I could not see anything built in for this.

What I want to do is receive a request on an API endpoint and immediately send a response object to the client. After sending the response object I want to then continue processing the request I received. 

I Have tried to create a thread and split the response and work I believe this will work locally but Lambda will terminate the process due to a response being sent. 
When using the pure lambda route, the method terminates when the response is returned  (locally)

So with that in mind...
Am I overlooking an obvious way to solve this using chalice?
Could I use the pure lambda mechanism to achieve this or will it also terminate its request early as it did locally?

The only way I have seen this work is to use the pure_lambda route but then generate a SNS topic to execute the pure lambda in the background, which seems cumbersome to me.

issue_title: Speed up "push" builds.
issue_body:
Travis has two build types that we use: "push" and "pull request". On a "pull request" commit the `TRAVIS_BRANCH` env. var. tells us what we are diff-ing against and makes it easy to limit the build jobs based on the changed files. In "push" builds, the best we have is `TRAVIS_COMMIT_RANGE` but this is not a very dependable value. I propose we rely instead on the GitHub API to determine the changed files. 1. Try our best to parse the PR number from the commit message (if this fails, just bail and run every single test) 2. Verify the PR number by checking if the `TRAVIS_COMMIT` (and the `HEAD` commit in our local checkout) matches the `merge_commit_sha` in the PR endpoint: https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pulls/2450 3. Once we have the PR, hit the PR files endpoint to get a list of changed files, e.g.: https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pulls/2461/files --- As an alternative to the first step, there is an "unpublished" API which helps associate a commit SHA with a PR. For example, see the parenthetical 2461 below: ![screen_shot_001](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/520669/19045350/3911fbb4-894d-11e6-8529-55f8d2fc272b.png) which tells us that 4e8afa8c3877376d7e9a6df79e6a272ae1cdfee7 came as part of #2461. The actual API returns an SVG with the payload containing ```
  • (#2461)
    • ``` The API simply requires the commit hash in `TRAVIS_COMMIT` (i.e. just the `HEAD` commit on our local checkout), e.g. https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/branch_commits/4e8afa8c3877376d7e9a6df79e6a272ae1cdfee7 --- @tseaver @jonparrott PTAL and LMKWYT FYI this will build on top of work done for #2277.

issue_title: Google cloud storage needs requests >= 2.18.0 but the setup.py only requires >= 2.4.0
issue_body:
According to the requests [changelog](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/community/updates/) the Response class was turned into a context manager only in version 2.18.0. However in google/resumable_media/requests/download.py line 62 (inside _write_to_stream) the code tries to use "response" as a context manager. Some digging reveals that this response makes its way from Client._http which is defined in google/cloud/client.py and gets the Response object from google/auth/transport/requests.py:AuthorizedSession.

So... it definitely feels like something in here needs to require requests >= 2.18.0 (maybe all of them?). 

Thoughts?

Detailed info:

1. OS type and version: N/A
2. Python version and virtual environment information: 2.7 but I don't think it matters.
3. google-cloud-python version: google-cloud-storage v 1.3.1
4. Stacktrace if available: below.
5. Steps to reproduce: Install a version of the requests library _less than 2.18.0_. Get some blob that exists. Try blob.download_as_string(). I personally re-pro'd this with version 2.10.0 to check. Note that the setup.py only requires 2.0.0 for storage and 2.4.0 for client, etc...

-- PG.

issue_title: ivy_utils.mapjars() can be  slow when invoked from ivy_resolve.execute()
issue_body:
I'm sorry I haven't had time to delve further. I wanted to capture this before I forgot about it because we've already changed our logic to avoid the problem.  My guess is that there is some code that is dead and should be removed.

We ran across this yesterday when working on resurrecting  *pants goal idea*.  We added some code that  called self.context.products.require('jar_dependencies') elsewhere that would trigger the inner loop in ivy_resolve.execute():

```
 create_jardeps_for = self.context.products.isrequired('jar_dependencies')
    if create_jardeps_for:
      genmap = self.context.products.get('jar_dependencies')
      for target in filter(create_jardeps_for, targets):
        self._ivy_utils.mapjars(genmap, target, executor=executor,
                                workunit_factory=self.context.new_workunit)
```

The result is that ivy is invoked many many times and its extremely slow... It takes minutes to bring up the IDE on some of our projects using this scheme because we have a lot of external dependencies.

Now, we observed that this code doesn't get called during a normal java compile.  It appears that instead a single ivy.xml file is created and all these dependencies are resolved in a single invocation of ivy.  There is a step named 'map_jars' from jvm_dependency_analyzer.py that prints out.  We ended up analyzing the way that works and started looking in 'ivy_jar_products' and got the mapping that way.

* should anyone be using ivy_utils.mapjars() ?  If not, let's nix it.
* if it is still useful, maybe we should change it ivy_resolve.execut() from using an inner loop that gets invoked many times to batching up the work and invoking it once.

issue_title: pants export format currently does not support jars resolved with classifiers
Python `test` is not always hermetic.
issue_body:
Although the python backend test task uses a chroot for dependency isolation, it manipulates the `PYTHONPATH` to adjoin code coverage libraries.  This can cause python path surprises when pants runs itself in a subprocess during tests.

Details of this sort of problem can be found in https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AURORA-1717 (towards bottom details become more relevant).

issue_title: GoBuildgen is lossy when re-generating non-gloabally in `--materialize` mode.
issue_body:
As reported by @dudeche:

For example, suppose a repo with many `go_binary` targets, some of which have various dependencies on aws/aws-sdk-go.  If `buildgen.go` is run against only a subset of those targets, the full dependency set on [aws/aws-sdk-go](https://github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go) may not be represented leading to improper dependency thinning, like so:
```
[10:27:04] danbenson@ObvsMBP36:[~/workspace/medium/mono-test]: git diff 3rdparty/golang/github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go/BUILD

This is because `SourceRoots.all_roots` hand-rolls ignores where it would probably be using a `ProjectTree`.  Some work to untangle `ProjectTree` instantiation from `goal_runner.py` is probably needed first.

One upshot of this is this [jenkins CI failure](http://jenkins.pantsbuild.org/job/pantsbuild/job/pants/branch/master/38/execution/node/426/log/):
```
...
[== 10:39 Installing and testing package pantsbuild.pants.contrib.go-1.1.0-pre0 ... ==]


13:44:41 00:00 [main]
               (To run a reporting server: ./pants server)
13:44:41 00:00   [setup]
13:44:41 00:00     [parse]
               Executing tasks in goals: buildgen -> bootstrap -> imports -> unpack-jars -> deferred-sources -> jvm-platform-validate -> gen -> resolve -> compile -> resources -> test
13:44:42 00:01   [buildgen]
13:44:42 00:01     [go]
                   buildgen.go ignoring targets passed on the command line and re-materializing the complete Go BUILD forest.
FAILURE: Can only BUILD gen for a single Go local sources source root, found:
	.cache/pantsbuild/pants/bin/go/mac/10.10/1.6.2/unpacked/go/src/go
	.home/.cache/pants/bin/go/mac/10.10/1.6.2/unpacked/go/src/go
	contrib/go/examples/src/go


13:44:43 00:02   [complete]
               FAILURE

Failed to install and test package pantsbuild.pants.contrib.go-1.1.0-pre0!

Dry run release failed.

Failed to create pants distributions.

Here the default options should be preventing `SourceRoots` from considering `.cache/` or `.home/` dirs:
```
$ ./pants options | grep pants_ignore
pants_ignore = ['.*', 'build-support/*.venv/'] (from CONFIG)
```

issue_title: Ability to use a place_id as a destination in the directions service
Address parsing issue
issue_body:
I've come across a parsing issue when the PlaceName and StreetName are the same, and there is a unit number at the end of the address string. While I can't post the actual address that I used, It is really easy to reproduce with a made up address.

```
>>> fails = '12345 CLEVELAND BOULEVARD #26, CLEVELAND OH 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(fails)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 94, in tag
    raise RepeatedLabelError(address_string, parse(address_string), label)
usaddress.RepeatedLabelError:

ERROR: Unable to tag this string because more than one area of the string has the same label

ORIGINAL STRING:  12345 CLEVELAND BOULEVARD #26, CLEVELAND OH 12345-6789
PARSED TOKENS:    [('12345', 'AddressNumber'), ('CLEVELAND', 'StreetName'), ('BOULEVARD', 'StreetNamePreType'), ('#', 'StreetName'), ('26,', 'StreetName'), ('CLEVELAND', 'PlaceName'), ('OH', 'StateName'), ('12345-6789', 'ZipCode')]
UNCERTAIN LABEL:  StreetName

When this error is raised, it's likely that either (1) the string is not a valid address or (2) some tokens were labeled incorrectly

To report an error in labeling a valid address, open an issue at https://github.com/datamade/usaddress/issues/new - it'll help us continue to improve usaddress!

For more information, see the documentation at http://usaddress.readthedocs.org/
```

These variants also fail:
```
'12345 CLEVELAND BOULEVARD #26, CLEVELAND OH 12345-6789'
'12345 TAMPA BOULEVARD #26, TAMPA FL 12345-6789'
```

However, the following address parses, showing that the repeated city name may be a factor.
```
>>> good = '12345 RIVER BOULEVARD #26, CLEVELAND OH 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetName', 'RIVER'), ('StreetNamePostType', 'BOULEVARD'), ('OccupancyIdentifier', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'CLEVELAND'), ('StateName', 'OH'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')
```

Also, clearly separating the occupancy identifier with a comma also resolves the issue:

```
>>> good = '12345 CLEVELAND BOULEVARD,  # 26, CLEVELAND OH 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetName', 'CLEVELAND'), ('StreetNamePostType', 'BOULEVARD'), ('OccupancyIdentifier', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'CLEVELAND'), ('StateName', 'OH'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')
```

Using the more mundane 'ROAD' instead of 'BOULEVARD' makes things parse without an error, but the results are clearly incorrect, with the Occupancy identifier shown as the street name:

```
>>> not_really_good = '12345 TAMPA ROAD #26, TAMPA FL 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(not_really_good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetNamePreType', 'TAMPA ROAD'), ('StreetName', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'TAMPA'), ('StateName', 'FL'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')

>>> not_really_good = '12345 TAMPA ROAD # 26, TAMPA FL 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(not_really_good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetNamePreType', 'TAMPA ROAD'), ('StreetName', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'TAMPA'), ('StateName', 'FL'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')

>>> not_really_good = '12345 CLEVELAND ROAD #26, CLEVELAND OH 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(not_really_good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetNamePreType', 'CLEVELAND ROAD'), ('StreetName', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'CLEVELAND'), ('StateName', 'OH'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')
```

Adding a comma between city and state also make it better:
```
>>> good = '12345 CLEVELAND BOULEVARD # 26, CLEVELAND, OH 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetName', 'CLEVELAND'), ('StreetNamePostType', 'BOULEVARD'), ('OccupancyIdentifier', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'CLEVELAND'), ('StateName', 'OH'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')
```

Using the 'BLVD' abbreviation will fix the problem as well:

```
>>> good = '12345 TAMPA BLVD #26, TAMPA FL 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetName', 'TAMPA'), ('StreetNamePostType', 'BLVD'), ('OccupancyIdentifier', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'TAMPA'), ('StateName', 'FL'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')
```

The street name appears to have to be a likely to be a well known city name to trigger the problem, as this parses.
```
>>> good = '12345 RIVER BOULEVARD #26, RIVER OH 12345-6789'
>>> usaddress.tag(good)
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', '12345'), ('StreetName', 'RIVER'), ('StreetNamePostType', 'BOULEVARD'), ('OccupancyIdentifier', '# 26'), ('PlaceName', 'RIVER'), ('StateName', 'OH'), ('ZipCode', '12345-6789')]), 'Street Address')
```

issue_title: SummaryLogs are not summarizing - new one created for each session.
issue_body:
## Summary

SummaryLogs are not accumulating progress over sessions, see an example of three consecutively created SummaryLogs, for the same user/channel/content combination.

```
    {
        "pk": 8,
        "user": 3,
        "content_id": "c1fef81ed45543cda589c53db04e8a9f",
        "channel_id": "838ca10504b4470db20767208aebf133",
        "start_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:16:40.307000Z",
        "end_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:16:43.481000Z",
        "completion_timestamp": null,
        "time_spent": 1472231803.481,
        "progress": 0.0,
        "kind": "audio",
        "extra_fields": "{}"
    },
    {
        "pk": 9,
        "user": 3,
        "content_id": "c1fef81ed45543cda589c53db04e8a9f",
        "channel_id": "838ca10504b4470db20767208aebf133",
        "start_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:16:43.628000Z",
        "end_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:17:00.687000Z",
        "completion_timestamp": null,
        "time_spent": 17.206,
        "progress": 0.0,
        "kind": "audio",
        "extra_fields": "{}"
    },
    {
        "pk": 10,
        "user": 3,
        "content_id": "c1fef81ed45543cda589c53db04e8a9f",
        "channel_id": "838ca10504b4470db20767208aebf133",
        "start_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:17:00.790000Z",
        "end_timestamp": "2016-08-26T17:17:01.199000Z",
        "completion_timestamp": null,
        "time_spent": 0.512,
        "progress": 0.0,
        "kind": "audio",
        "extra_fields": "{}"
    },
```

This is happening because the `idKey` static property of the ContentSummaryLog (and incidentally, the ContentSessionLog) are not currently set to `pk`, but are setting to the default `id`. However, in the serializers.py, we are sending the id field as `pk`.

Flagging @jamalex @jayoshih here to alert to issue. But this will be fixed in a PR I will be submitting later today.
## System information

_Please specify the Kolibri version you were using and your operating system!_
- Version: MVP
- Operating system: Linux
- Browser: Chrome
## Traceback or relevant snippet from server.log or browser console

```
INSERT TRACEBACK, LOG MESSAGES ETC. HERE
```
## How to reproduce
1. Engage with a piece of content while logged in.
2. Navigate away.
3. Navigate back.
4. Engage again.
## Real-life consequences (anything community should be aware of, for instance how it affects your deployment)

SummaryLogs are not working as intended, and are currently doing no more than SessionLogs.

issue_title: Bootstrapping python packages
issue_body:
So I see this project is still pretty bare-bones :)

It's worth getting things on the right track from Day 1 because experience proves that things get harder and less prioritized as projects grow.

I have a couple of questions to start out with:
- Should it be py2+3?
- Should dependencies be bundled in as with Kolibri?
- What kind of dependency policy do we otherwise impose (like pure-python etc)

Otherwise, what I'm looking for is:
- Packaging (setup.py)
- CI
- Code coverage
- Docs w/ sphinx-apidocs

The tool we can use is called [cookiecutter](https://github.com/audreyr/cookiecutter-pypackage) (the link is for the specific cookie cutter template). It's a good idea to check out the forks of cookiecutter-pypackage because people sometimes come up with cool variations that don't get merged upstream because the maintainer doesn't agree.

issue_title: Specifying `chunk_size` when saving images results in a max chunk of size 32768, with wrap-around
issue_body:
The docstring in `docker.models.images.Image#save` states that 'data will be streamed as it is received. Default: 2 MB'. This default is from `docker.constants#DEFAULT_DATA_CHUNK_SIZE` which is set to 1024 * 2048, or 2MB. 

However, when iterating through the 'chunked' results, each chunk is cut off at 32768 bytes per, no matter what the `chunk_size` setting is. 

`
# using default of "2 MB"
>> import docker
>> d = docker.from_env() 
>> for chunk in d.images.get('alpine:3.7').save():
>>     print("chunk of size {}".format(len(chunk)))
chunk of size 32768
chunk of size 32768
... 
# continues to iterate until hitting image size ~4 MB
...
chunk of size 27136
# ^ last remaining chunk 

# using a smaller size than 32768
>> import docker
>> d = docker.from_env() 
>> for chunk in d.images.get('alpine:3.7').save(chunk_size=32724): # 40 less bytes than max
>>     print("chunk of size {}".format(len(chunk)))
chunk of size 32728
chunk of size 40
chunk of size 32728
chunk of size 40
... 
# wrap-around continues 
...
`

Since the example given in the docstring concatenates the chunks into a file, this behavior doesn't cause issues there. However, if anyone actually wants to specify a chunk size, such as to encrypt the chunks into a file, they'll be in for a surprise with the wrap-around behavior. 

I'm guessing the hard limit is due to response size constraints in either the Docker client or server, but either way this behavior should be addressed. Either docs reflect the actual behavior or the client api should pipe the results as described. Unless I'm missing something here... which is totally possible.

issue_title: Reverse protocol handling for WebSocketResource
issue_body:
I have a set of unit tests (in https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole) that recently started failing when I combine autobahn-0.13.1 and Twisted trunk. It looks like https://github.com/twisted/twisted/commit/4f9e36d8ad21f9d73369f4d506bab30f61c2ad71 (landed 29-Mar-2016) is where the problem started (that's the first revision after Twisted-16.1.1 was branched off that causes my project's tests to fail).

The commit says:

    Merge hide-request-transport-8191-5: Hide the transport backing the HTTPChannel object from twisted.web Resource objects.

And my tests are using `WebSocketResource` to put a websocket on a specific URL. I haven't tracked down the specific failure yet, but it seems like the server is having an internal error when the client attempts to connect to it, or the server isn't accepting the connection at all.

The relevant Twisted bug is at https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/8191 .

When I get some time (maybe next week), I'll figure out how to run autobahn's test suite against various versions of Twisted and see what happens.
