issue_title: Slow check of DSC step signature
issue_body:
It has been observed that for [this regression benchmark](https://github.com/stephenslab/dsc-reg/tree/4ca90b28c77d0c26084def33578bd62b941f70a9), it takes ~500 sec to run `--replicate 20`, but to rerun without any code change it takes ~70 sec. Notice that ~70 sec is also what `--replicate 1` takes, so this rules out the slowness checking on file signatures. 

That brings back to another SoS design that has led to this bottleneck. Long story short is that this design was added a while ago to exclusively address to DSC's typical usage of running large amount of small jobs on a single machine. But there was certain implementation hurdles due to Python's GIL (compared to a typical SoS task that does not run inside Python child processes) so we always have trouble limiting maximum running processeses. This is a known Python multiprocessing module problem. We ended up having to use file lock for each CPU thread. That is why at the beginning of each module block (can contain many module instances) there will be a brief halt for lock releases. But the delay is invariant to the number of instances in the module (`--replicate`).

We can try improving SoS engineering. In the mean time, here is a temp solution:

1. Have a fresh run of `dsc file.dsc --replicate 20`
2. For the specific module you have added, just run `dsc file.dsc --target "simulate * varbvs * score" --replicate 20`. This will be faster but will rebuild the query database to a smaller one.
3. After you are done with everything, run `dsc file.dsc --replicate --skip all`. With `--skip all` it will skip all the running part, but just build the query database.

Have not tested it a lot, but this is designed to work at least ... Maybe give it a shot?

issue_title: Explore the possible addition of a plugin framework to extend CLI functionality with customizations
issue_body:
I have verbalized this idea, but never written it down.

I want private plugins for administrative functionality against the APIs, plugins available for new/burgeoning APIs and products, and a clean integration point for third-party APIs which rely on Globus Auth so that they can piggy-back on our work to create a clean and beautiful CLI with minimal effort.
It also exposes the possibility of reorganizing existing functionality into a very limited core, combined with plugins for each service (which we'd probably never do, but it would be a cool architecture).

The basic idea is to do something like the following:
- Define an empty package, `globus_cli.plugins`
- Modify the main CLI method to load each subpackage of `globus_cli.plugins`
  - Well-behaved plugins will use a registration method at import time to inject themselves, potentially basic `click` command attachment
- Expose a carefully crafted `globus_cli.tools` or `globus_cli.api` module which targets plugin writers
- Define any functionality which we want or have a use for, but don't want in the core CLI, as a plugin, and keep a clean separation of core product concerns from "handy things that people need"
- Document the API layer for plugin development, providing at least one complete example

I've already seen several places where this would have been useful:
- If plugins can expand the set of scopes and resource servers used, new products in development can define plugins
- Globus Publish has a CLI, but would prefer full integration with the rest of Globus, and not to rewrite login code and other common "core" features
- Private plugins for administrative utilities including B32 decoding of unix usernames derived from UUIDs, endpoint management, and Secret Things of Great Secrecy
- Ports of functionality in the Hosted CLI (e.x. #150 ) which may not belong in the CLI long-term but have no true deprecation schedule

---

The questions that bother me are:
- We would be wrapping `click` for large portions of the tooling, so we need to be careful not to expose `click` directly to plugin authors
- If a plugin fails to tightly specify a required CLI version, how likely is it to still work? Will our internal API be stable?
- What does the maintenance burden on this look like? How likely will plugin support be to impair normal development?
  - I think this is minimal if we isolate plugins well, but maybe I'm too optimistic
- How much code complexity does this add to the CLI? Is it worth it even without maintenance concerns?
  - This, to me, is the biggest concern
- Can a broken plugin cause core `globus` commands to fail? Is this a major blocker?
  - I think it will always be possible for a sufficiently broken plugin to ruin the whole python environment, let alone the CLI
- Do we want to lazily load plugins ([example from click docs](http://click.pocoo.org/5/commands/#custom-multi-commands))? If so are there significant risks in defining a custom `click.MultiCommand` which also looks for registered plugin commands?
  - This is probably the "right way" but not the quick and easy path. Directly attaching plugin commands would get us over the hump and could be exposed via an API compatible with lazy loading
  - I think an alternative MultiCommand is actually really easy. The [whole implementation of click.Group](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/master/click/core.py#L1139-L1189) is only 50 lines, and half of that is convenience utilities. The only tricky bit is command chaining, which I think we can safely just disallow everywhere because its semantics are confusing and not of interest to us.

issue_title: conda env update --prune reports conflicts for removed packages (4.4)
issue_body:
[environments.zip](https://github.com/conda/conda/files/1671754/environments.zip) **I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior We have a Python 2 and Python 3 branch for our codebase while we're transitioning, with a corresponding env.yaml file for each. For a variety of reasons, some packages are not in both environments. Obviously all common packages have a different version due to requiring a different version of Python. When using `conda env update -n environment --prune --file env.yaml` command to sync an environment from our Python 2 branch to our Python 3 branch, conda reports a package conflict. This error is new since upgrading from 4.3 to 4.4.7. ### Steps to Reproduce I've attached a minimal working example for both .yaml files. ``` $ conda env update --file bug_env.yaml --prune -n test $ conda env update --file bug_env2.yaml --prune -n test Solving environment: failed UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict: - singledispatch==3.4.0.3=py36_0 - win_inet_pton==1.0.1=py27_0 Use "conda info " to see the dependencies for each package. ``` ## Expected Behavior Packages that have been removed from env.yaml (like win_inet_pton in this case) should be removed from the environment when run with --prune, and should not conflict. >>> es = Elasticsearch(...)

issue_title: AzureStack: images aliases is not applicable for azurestack
issue_body:
### Description
CLI has a set of aliases for the images referred from the following location
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/master/arm-compute/quickstart-templates/aliases.json

These aliases makes the assumption that these images are available in all the clouds and matches the 
definition like below
          "UbuntuLTS":{
            "publisher":"Canonical",
            "offer":"UbuntuServer",
            "sku":"16.04-LTS",
            "version":"latest"
          }

In azure stack , the alias does not make much sense as the admin may not have uploaded the same image
in azure stack the Ubuntu server sku could be 14.04.3 instead of 16.04-LTS

luckily , the image parameter can be specified with the whole urn like below and this is what being used in azurestack 
Canonical:UbuntuServer:14.04.3-LTS:1.0.0

All the public documentation is referring to the use of aliases and the aliases most likely wont work against azurestack unless the admin chooses to upload image matching the azure definitions

issue_title: Uploader fails if the full path to the target file doesn't exist
issue_body: Creation of a file in ADLS does not require the underlying path to exist. The create call itself will create the entire path or throw if it can't. The recommendation is to remove this requirement and fail only if the target file itself can't be created (which should still be tested for at the very beginning).

issue_title: functools.partial breaks new-style middleware
issue_body:
## Long story short

Applying `functools.partial` on a new-style middleware forces the application to treat it as an old-style middleware which sends the `application` and `handler` parameter to the middleware function instead of `request` and `handler`

## Expected behaviour

Applying `functools.partial` on the new-style middleware should not "convert" it to an old style one

## Steps to reproduce

``` python3
from functools import partial
from aiohttp import web


@web.middleware
async def test_middleware(request, handler, *, config):
    print(request.headers, config)
    return await handler(request)


async def hello_word(request):
    return web.Response('Hello World')


def build_app():
    app = web.Application()
    app.middlewares.append(partial(test_middleware, config=1))

    app.router.add_route('GET', '/', hello_word)

    return app

if __name__ == '__main__':
    web.run_app(build_app())
```

Accessing `http://127.0.0.1:8080` will throw:
```
Error handling request
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/web_protocol.py", line 381, in start
    resp = await self._request_handler(request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/web_app.py", line 320, in _handle
    handler = await m(app, handler)
  File "test.py", line 7, in test_middleware
    print(request.headers, config)
AttributeError: 'Application' object has no attribute 'headers'
```

## Your environment
python = 3.6.5
aiohttp = 3.1.3

issue_title: Computing value and grad and hvp in one go
issue_body:
Given some function `fun`, I'd like to calculate `fun(x)`, `grad(fun)(x)` and `hessian_vector_product(fun)(x, v)` all in one go.

I've come up with an implementation but it's quite ugly
```python
def val_and_grad_and_hvp(fun):
    """
    Evaluates fun(x), grad(fun)(x) and hvp(fun)(x, v) in one go.

    I've copied the idea in autograd.differential_operators.grad_and_aux to
    implement this but there ought to be a much nicer way...
    """
    saved = lambda: None
    def vghvp(x, v):
        def grad_and_val(x):
            vjp, val = make_vjp(fun)(x)
            saved.val = val
            return vjp(1.)
        hvpfun, grad = make_vjp(grad_and_val)(x)
        return saved.val._value, grad, hvpfun(v)
    return vghvp
```
Do you think we can make this a bit nicer? It would be great if it were possible to avoid the trick of saving the value as a function's attribute, but maybe this can't be done with our current make_vjp API...

issue_title: Transaction nature of reactive framework needs to be explained better.
Move pypuppetdb/api/__init__.py to pypuppetdb
issue_body:
Since we no longer support API versioning there is little point in maintaining the `api` directory under `pypuppetdb`. Theremore we should move the file `pypuppetdb/api/__init__.py` to `pypuppetdb/BaseAPI.py`

All references to `pypuppetdb.api.BaseAPI()` will have to be updated to `pypuppetdb.BaseAPI()`

issue_title: How do wrappers force all results up to the first error?
issue_body:
I was thinking about this as I was writing up #142 and realized there seems to be no *public* API way to accomplish this. That is, how does one use a wrapper to stuff all the results up to when the first `hookimpl` fails? Currently `_Result.get_result()` will raise and `_Result.result` is being deprecated so the only way I can think of currently is using the internal `_result` instance var:

```python
@hookimpl(hookwrapper=True):
    outcome = yield
    if test_for_exc_to_swallow(outcome.excinfo):
        outcome.force_result(outcome._result)
        oucome._excinfo = None
```

Maybe this is just a naughty use case and we shouldn't be concerned?

issue_title: check_basket_is_valid recalculates purchase info of a basket Line
issue_body:
I work on an application where price calculation is dynamic and expense computationally. While trying to optimize my application code, I found that `check_basket_is_valid` does not use the cached `purchase_info` property available for basket lines. Because of this, I have certain Django views that are doing redundant work.

Since it calls `fetch_for_line` directly, it bypasses the proxying work and cache that `Line.purchase_info` provides.

issue_title: ResourceManager in .NET core does not fallback to unspecfic resource when using an unlocalized culture
issue_body:
Version: .NET Core App 1.1
OS Windows 7 (German)
Assembly System.Resources.ResourceManager, Version=4.0.0.0 
from %userprofile%\.nuget\packages\system.resources.resourcemanager\4.3.0\ref\netstandard1.0\System.Resources.ResourceManager.dll

I have a simple Resource named TestRes.resx plus three culture specific resources TestRes.de.resx, TestRes.en.resx and TestRes.it.resx.
There is one single key named "Test" with values indicating the used culture
Value from "de" resx
Value from "en" resx
Value from "it" resx
Value from unspecific resx.

Everything works just fine as long a I am using those cultures for which resx files do exist. But when calling GetString with another culture (i.e. "fr") the result (on my German OS) is: _Value from "de" resx_ instead of _Value from unspecific resx_

This behaviour is different from the .NET framework where using a culture for which no resx is given yields the value from the "default" resx (that without a culture suffix).

Am I missing something? Is this by design?

I've attached a zip file demonstrating the issue. There are two projects: a .NET Core Console App. The second one is a NET461 console app I added for comparison.

[LocalizationIssue.zip](https://github.com/dotnet/core/files/1128574/LocalizationIssue.zip)

issue_title: Synchronous Functions Should Release the GIL
issue_body:
Description
===========
Some functions that take a timeout do not release the GIL. This will cause all python threads to block until the function returns.

Functions that seem to do this include:
Consumer.committed()
Consumer.get_watermark_offsets()
Consumer.offsets_for_times()
Producer.flush()

Consumer.get_watermark_offsets() is the only function I'm completely sure of. The others are just a guess from looking at the code.

How to reproduce
================
Create a topic with one partition. Run the below example. Once the example has consumed a few messages, kill the broker hosting the partition. Notice the other thread is longer printing it's debug message.

```python
import time
import sys
import threading
import confluent_kafka
from confluent_kafka import Consumer, KafkaError
from uuid import uuid4

def debug_thread_func():
    while True:
        print("Test...")
        time.sleep(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(confluent_kafka.version(), confluent_kafka.libversion())

    debug_thread = threading.Thread(target=debug_thread_func)
    debug_thread.start()

    client = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'gateway:9092', 'group.id': str(uuid4()),
              'default.topic.config': {'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'}})

    def assigned(consumer, partitions):
        print("Assigned:", partitions)
        

    client.subscribe(['ibbot'], on_assign=assigned)

    while True:
        msg = client.poll(timeout=1)
        for partition in client.assignment():
            print(client.get_watermark_offsets(partition))

        if msg is not None:
            if msg.error():
                print("Error: ", msg.error())
            else:
                print("Data")

    client.close()
   

```

Note that this example is probably dependent on a bug in rdkafka, so it may not be reproducible 100% of the time. You can also reproduce this with a multi-partition and broker setup. In this case, the application only deadlocks for as long as it takes for a new leader to be elected.


Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [x] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`):  kafka-python: ('0.11.4', 721920)  rdkafka: ('0.11.4', 722175)
 - [x] Apache Kafka broker version: 0.11.0.2
 - [x] Client configuration: `{'bootstrap.servers': 'gateway:9092', 'group.id': str(uuid4()),
              'default.topic.config': {'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'}}`
 - [X] Operating system: RHEL 7
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [ ] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [ ] Critical issue

issue_title: Parser parses incorrectly in British Summer Time
issue_body:
**The Problem**
Tests fail in British Summer Time (BST).

**To Reproduce**
Set timezone on your computer to BST and run tests. This is confirmed to happen on both MacOS and Ubuntu, on both Python2 and Python3.

**Investigation**

On the Python interpreter in any timezone that is not BST (nor GMT):

```
>>> import dateutil.parser
>>> date = 'Fri, 14 Apr 2017 16:18:53 GMT'
>>> dateutil.parser.parse(date)
datetime.datetime(2017, 4, 14, 16, 18, 53, tzinfo=tzutc())
```

GMT parses to `tzutc()` as expected.

In BST and GMT:

```
>>> import dateutil.parser
>>> date = 'Fri, 14 Apr 2017 16:18:53 GMT'
>>> dateutil.parser.parse(date)
datetime.datetime(2017, 4, 14, 16, 18, 53, tzinfo=tzlocal())
```

GMT now parses to `tzlocal()`, which is not correct if you're in BST, since BST is one hour off of GMT.

issue_title: Wrong dst() value for time in Portugal tz
issue_body:
Pick a time in the Summer of 1996:
```
>>> t = datetime(1996, 6, 1, tzinfo=tz.gettz('Portugal'))
```
the time zone abbreviation is computed correctly:
```
>>> t.tzname()
'WEST'
```
([WEST](http://www.timeanddate.com/time/zones/west) = Western European Summer Time).

However, 

```
>>> t.dst()
datetime.timedelta(0)
```

and as a consequence:

```
>>> t.timetuple().tm_isdst
0
>>> from time import strftime
>>> strftime('%Z', t.timetuple())
'WET'
```

The problem is that `t.dst()` is computed as a difference between `t.utcoffset()` and the offset of the last standard time before `t`, but Portugal switched to `WEST` in the Spring of 1996 from `CET`, not `WET` and therefore UTC offset did not change, while isdst did.

issue_title: Fix build failure problem
issue_body:
There is an intermittent build failure issue caused by some interaction with `updatezinfo.py` and the IANA time zone servers. It's not clear (to me) why we get so many failures, but it may have something to do with some rate-limiting that they haven't told us about.

I think the initial fix to this is to add a "wait-retry" loop to the Travis and Appveyor CI configurations. Longer term, I'm thinking we may want to separate out the timezone metadata and rehost the tzfiles at that repo, and the IANA server can be considered a mirror.

issue_title: tempfile._TemporaryFileWrapper not available under Google Appengine
issue_body:
Google appengine replaces the tempfile module with a custom one (since you can't write to the FS under "traditional" GAE): https://code.google.com/p/googleappengine/source/browse/trunk/python/google/appengine/dist/tempfile.py

However this module lacks the `_TemporaryFileWrapper` on which `utils.DownloadedFile` is based: https://github.com/core-api/python-client/blob/master/coreapi/utils.py#L119

Given that `_TemporaryFileWrapper` is an undocumented API (class) IMHO the dependency on it should be removed.

issue_title: Support for sorts with constructors in Datalog is incomplete
issue_body:
A [recent commit](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/commit/2cbeedec690fbd89da1a4154ae96708af77cfe65) by @NikolajBjorner adds support for sorts with constructors in Datalog relations. Unfortunately, it appears to be incomplete. The following example illustrates two issues I've encountered so far. 

```
(set-option :fixedpoint.engine datalog)
(define-sort s () (_ BitVec 3))
(declare-datatypes () ( (Edge (ed (from s) (to s)) nil ) ))
(declare-rel edge (Edge))
(declare-rel path (s s))
(declare-var f s)
(declare-var t s)
(declare-var g s)
(declare-var e Edge)

;(rule (=> (and (edge e) (is-ed e)) (path (from e) (to e) )))
(rule (=> (edge (ed f t)) (path f t)))

(rule (=> (and (path f g) (path g t)) (path f t)))
(rule (edge (ed #b001 #b101)))
(rule (edge (ed #b101 #b111)))
(query path :print_answer true)
(query edge :print_answer true)
```

Issue 1: The first query above succeeds and prints the answer, while the second query fails, refusing to print a non-numeric sort:

```
sat
(or (and (= (:var 0) #b101) (= (:var 1) #b111))
    (and (= (:var 0) #b001) (= (:var 1) #b101))
    (and (= (:var 0) #b001) (= (:var 1) #b111)))
(error "query failed: sort 'Edge' is not recognized as a sort that contains numeric values.
Use Bool, BitVec, Int, Real, or a Finite domain sort")
unknown
```

Issue 2: The commented-out rule is logically equivalent to the one following it; however uncommenting this rule and removing the following line causes both queries to fail: 

```
(error "query failed: Uninterpreted 'from' in path(#1,#2) :- 
 edge(#0),
 (= (:var 2) (to (:var 0))),
 (= (:var 1) (from (:var 0))),
 (is-ed (:var 0)).
")
unknown
(error "query failed: Uninterpreted 'from' in path(#1,#2) :- 
 edge(#0),
 (= (:var 2) (to (:var 0))),
 (= (:var 1) (from (:var 0))),
 (is-ed (:var 0)).
")
unknown
```

issue_title: dims check for auto-generated extension classes
issue_body:
I would like classes that are auto-generated by extensions to automatically check that dimensions with the same label across datasets have the same length. If they do not, either throw an error or a warning. For example, in the code below, `dset1` and `dset2` are in `DimsTest` and both have a dimension named `'cells'`. I would like it to be enforced that the lengths of these datasets must be equal. The code demonstrates that this is not currently the case, since there is no warning or error when I set `dset1=[0, 1, 2, 3], dset2=[0, 1, 2]`. I would prefer if this check occurred in the `__init__` of `NWBDataInterface`, as opposed to on write or in a separate validator.


```python
from datetime import datetime

from pynwb.spec import (NWBGroupSpec, NWBDatasetSpec, NWBNamespaceBuilder)
from pynwb import get_class, load_namespaces, NWBHDF5IO, NWBFile


project_name = 'dims_test'
ns_path = project_name + '.namespace.yaml'
ext_source = project_name + '.extensions.yaml'
ns_builder = NWBNamespaceBuilder(project_name, project_name)

datasets = [
    NWBDatasetSpec(doc='dset1', dtype='uint32',shape=(None,),
                   name='dset1', dims=('cells',)),
    NWBDatasetSpec(doc='dset2', dtype='uint64', shape=(None,),
                   name='dset2', dims=('cells',))
]

cont_data = NWBGroupSpec(doc='data',
                         datasets=datasets,
                         neurodata_type_inc='NWBDataInterface',
                         neurodata_type_def='DimsTest')

ns_builder.add_spec(ext_source, cont_data)
ns_builder.export(ns_path)

##

load_namespaces(ns_path)
DimsTest = get_class('DimsTest', project_name)

dims_test = DimsTest(name='name', source='source', dset1=[0, 1, 2, 3], dset2=[0, 1, 2])

nwbfile = NWBFile(source='source', session_description='session_description',
                  identifier='identifier', session_start_time=datetime.now(),
                  file_create_date=datetime.now())

module = nwbfile.create_processing_module(name='name',
                                          source='source',
                                          description='description')
module.add_container(dims_test)

with NWBHDF5IO('dims_test.nwb', mode='w') as io:
    io.write(nwbfile)
```

issue_title: Improve performance using select_related
issue_body:
I set up a ModelResource to export a number of fields off of ForeignKey relationships and noticed that performance was quite a bit worse than it could be (over 3x worse, in my case), since each relationship had to be fetched separately.

It seems to me that `get_queryset` could be updated to use [`select_related`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/models/querysets/#select-related). One approach would be to add `select_related` as an attribute to ResourceOptions, but since it already has the list of column names, it should be able to automatically determine which related fields are needed. Something like this:

```python
        # Get column names of related fields
        needed_relations = [f.column_name for f in self.get_fields() if '__' in f.column_name]

        # Strip the foreign key field name; we want just the tables
        needed_relations = [f.rpartition('__')[0] for f in needed_relations]

        queryset = queryset.select_related(*needed_relations)
```

Does this approach seem reasonable? (I'm still relatively new to Django and this project, so please let me know if I'm missing something.) If the general idea is sound, then I can submit a proper PR.

issue_title: Do we need to support get_all with a wildcard parent_id?
issue_body:
While investigating #1507, @peterbe discovered that the `DISTINCT` doesn't really make sense unless you have a wildcard `parent_id`. He proved this to his own satisfaction by removing `DISTINCT` and observing that no tests break.

Under what circumstances do we even have a wildcard `parent_id`? Unlike `delete`, where you might want to delete a thing and all its children, I couldn't think of a way to invoke this mechanism using the HTTP API. Indeed, there's a subtle bug in the current query and the fact that nobody has reported it makes me suspect that nobody ever actually uses it. Can we get rid of it?

issue_title: Calling model_admin.get_queryset with test Request breaks plugins
issue_body:
In utils.py, line 183, you create a fake request object, and pass this to model_admin.get_queryset() on line 186.

However, this ignores changes made to the request object in Django middleware. In particular, some plugins, such as Django-Guardian, require the request object to pass through the site middleware.

Django-Guardian, a row-level permissions app, expects to find the user attribute. Because you are passing it a fake Request object, the "user" attribute does not exist.

I fixed the problem by changing utils.get_model_queryset, adding an additional parameter: context, and retrieving the actual Request object from the context.

I then pass the context to utils.get_model_queryset in templatetages/jet_tags.jet_sibling_object_url()

However, there still seem to be issues with how the Guardian templates are interacting with the Jet templates that I have yet to resolve. In particular, CSRF is not passed reliably into the Guardian templates, and the User menu in the upper left is garbled.

issue_title: Add clarification around concurrency and parallelization
issue_body:
Hey guys,

I've been wondering what is the concurrency behavior / story of graphene but I don't see anything mentioned in the code base. 

A few questions on my mind:
- Has it been a design constrain yet at all? Was the API / architecture designed with this potential concurrency down the road?
- Can any schema be resolved in parallel in a thread pool? like most sql drivers do
- Can we also use gevent or twisted for I/O calls that support it? like making network calls
- Can we mix and match the 2 approaches?

Since Python does not really shine out-of-the-box when it comes to parallelization, I think having clarity would help contributors to build performant integrations with different frameworks. Also it would help users to decide to use Graphene or not.

In my case, I could potentially use the base NodeJS implementation to call over RPCs into my python services, but we are mostly a python shop and I don't want to introduce yet another language (esp Node :p ) unless I really need to.

If concurrency hasn't been given yet much thought but you think it should, especially in the v1 context, I would be very happy to brainstorm here with the broader community :)

Cheers,
J

issue_title: Bidirectional relationships require a specific load order
DjangoFilterConnectionField does not accept Connections as type

Standard ConnectionFields allow you to pass a custom Connection in as the type for the field. This allows for defining Connection Fields like `totalCount` etc...

DjangoFilterConnectionField does not currently support this.

issue_title: Multiplication error in convolution product
issue_body:
Hi !
I'm trying to code a fast convolution product function in Cython. I have this code with Numpy, which works well except it's awefully slow :

```
cdef np.ndarray[DTYPE_t, ndim=2] convolve(np.ndarray[DTYPE_t, ndim=2] a, np.ndarray[DTYPE_t, ndim=2] b, str domain):
    cdef int MK =  b.shape[0]
    cdef int NK = b.shape[1]
    cdef int M = a.shape[0]
    cdef int N = a.shape[1]

    #cdef np.ndarray[np.complex64_t, ndim=2] ahat, bhat, chat
    #cdef np.ndarray[DTYPE_t, ndim=2] c

    ahat = np.fft.rfft2(a, (M + MK -1, N + NK -1))#.output_array
    bhat = np.fft.rfft2(b, (M + MK -1, N + NK -1))#.output_array

    cdef int Y, X

    if domain =="same":
        Y = M
        X = N
    elif domain == "valid":
        Y = M - MK + 1
        X = N - NK + 1
    elif domain == "full":
        Y = M + MK - 1
        X = N + NK - 1
    else:
        raise SyntaxError

    chat = ahat * bhat
    c = np.fft.irfft2(chat, (Y, X)).astype(np.float32)#.output_array

    return c
```

Now if I use the pyFFTW version:

```

    elif domain == "full":
        Y = M + MK - 1
        X = N + NK - 1
    else:
        raise SyntaxError

    chat = ahat * bhat

Currently it looks like this when we have to choose which account role to assume:

```
$ aws-google-auth --idp-id xxxxxxx --sp-id 6666666 --username user.name@domain.com --duration 43200 --ask-role
Failed to import U2F libraries, U2F login unavailable. Other methods can still continue.
Google Password:
Open the Google App, and tap 'Yes' on the prompt to sign in ...
[  1] arn:aws:iam::111111:role/sso/sso-administrator
[  2] arn:aws:iam::22222:role/sso/sso-administrator
[  3] arn:aws:iam::333333:role/sso/sso-administrator
[  4] arn:aws:iam::44444:role/sso/sso-administrator
Type the number (1 - 4) of the role to assume: 4
Assuming arn:aws:iam::4444444:role/sso/sso-administrator
Credentials Expiration: 2018-08-02 19:19:07+02:00
```

Problem is that these ARNs are not very informative as one would have to remember the AWS account number.

Is it possible to show the account alias?

When you do Google SSO auth (using SAML) to the AWS console, it shows the account aliases like this:

```
Account: my-account-1 (1111111)
Account: my-account-2 (22222)
etc
```

issue_title: Check unchecked types in certbot-dns-digitalocean
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-digitalocean` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_digitalocean.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation! 

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase. 

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!). 

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: Produce python patch to make python-acme 0.4.1 compatible with 'adding-random-entries-to-the-directory'
issue_body:
The addition  as by is making incompatible python-acme 0.4.1 with any ubuntu/debian distribution that is still offering it as official package.

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/adding-random-entries-to-the-directory/33417

this include making impossible to use python-acme on ubuntu-trusty, ubuntu-xenial, debian-jessie, debian-stretch that are the long term support platforms fro Ubuntu and Debian.

This ticket is to propose to produce a patch to make python-acme compatible with 'adding-random-entries-to-the-directory' addition to lets'encrypt spec.

issue_title: Properly handle installing certs/keys into an SSL vhost without cert and key directives
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

Ubuntu 16.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

Dev instructions

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

Starting with the default Apache config on this system, I removed the `SSLCertificateFile` and `SSLCertificateKeyFile` directives from `/etc/apache2/sites-available/default-ssl.conf` and put them in `/etc/apache2/apache2.conf`. Then I ran:
```
sudo rm /etc/apache2/sites-*/000-default.conf
sudo a2enmod ssl
sudo a2ensite default-ssl.conf
sudo certbot -d example.com --apache
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

This configuration is technically valid so we should ideally support it. The problem is we mark the vhost as SSL and assume cert and key directives exist in the file when they technically could be in the top level configuration.

issue_title: Modify GitHub URL in letsencrypt-auto
issue_body:
There is no rush to do this. I verified for #2639 that `letsencrypt-auto` will still receive updates after the repo rename. Changing the URL in `letsencrypt-auto` should only be done for aesthetic.

If we don't rename the repo until shortly before the rename, I believe we should wait to do this until 0.7.0 to avoid making last minute changes.

issue_title: Wrong vhost selected for redirect
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 16.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
Developer instructions

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
I extracted [these files](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/files/927046/example.tar.gz) into the sites-available directory in Apache, ran `certbot --apache --redirect` and selected all domains.

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
The only redirect was created in `example.conf` which is incorrect. I believe there's an issue with vhost selection here where the `*.example.org` is being used despite there being a vhost with a `ServerName`/`ServerAlias` directive with the specific name we're trying to enable a redirect for.

cc @joohoi, @sagi

issue_title: Comma not escaped in username
issue_body:
I'm using Active Directory LDAP with following attributes:
dn: `CN=Doe\, John,CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com`
cn: `Doe, John`

My configuration is the following:
`c.LDAPAuthenticator.allowed_groups = ['CN={username},CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com'`
`c.LDAPAuthenticator.lookup_dn_user_dn_attribute = 'cn'`

It seems the authentication fails since the comma in the cn is not escaped [which is required for AD](https://social.technet.microsoft.com/wiki/contents/articles/5312.active-directory-characters-to-escape.aspx) and therefore doesn't match the dn. I get the following reported in the log:
```
[D 2018-03-02 20:47:41.249 JupyterHub ldapauthenticator:278] Attempting to bind jdoe with CN=Doe, John,CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com
[D 2018-03-02 20:47:41.272 JupyterHub ldapauthenticator:328] Status of user bind jdoe with CN=Doe, John,CN=Users,DC=mycompany,DC=com : False
    LDAPBindError: automatic bind not successful - invalidCredentials
[
```



My guess is the escape_userdn_if_needed() function needs to handle this case since the ldap3 escape_filter_chars does not escape the comma.

```
def escape_userdn_if_needed(self, userdn):
        if self.escape_userdn:
            return escape_filter_chars(userdn)
        else:
            return userdn
```

If there's a way around this problem by simply changing the configuration please let me know.

issue_title: Get rid of all usages of `assetAddresses` and `channelManagerAddresses`
issue_body:
In https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/441d7637bc89cc7d70c0afa2cc3817b400fe768e/raiden/smart_contracts/Registry.sol

Currently `assetAddresses()` is used to get a list of all registered asset addresses. 

`channelManagerAddresses()` uses above function to get all channel addresses

Since we want to move to only using mappings instead of arrays, these two functions should no longer be used in the python code base. Instead some way of building the lists of open channels and registered assets should be implemented. This could be done by iterating through the BC for relevant events.

This issue is part of splitting up #232 in to smaller issues

issue_title: Allow `--log-file` to overwrite the hardcoded raiden-debug.log
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

As mentioned by @palango [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/2010#issuecomment-409150321) we currently don't allow the user to changed the hardcoded `raiden-debug.log`.

That should change. Having debug file on by default now that we are testing is sensible but the user should be able to disable if if they want.


## Task

Add a `--disable-debug-logfile` argument, as per our RC discussion.

issue_body:
I was just trying to pywebpush on appengine and one of the problems I was hitting is an issue with cryptography module:

    ImportError: No module named cryptography.hazmat.bindings._constant_time

After googling it is either an issue with my setup (which generally has mixed solutions), or it could be appengine running in a weird environment / container that means the compiled library for my machine fails to work on App Engine.

Looking at the docs for AppEngine it looks like the make crypto library is pycrypto. I was just wondering if that could* be an option for this library?

I'm not a Python developer so if cryptography is the main module developers use then please close this issue, but if there is no strong opinion would you be open to taking a PR to switch?

issue_title: SoS frontend sometimes use patched execute function only for the second cell
issue_body:
This is because

```
        events.on('execute.CodeCell', wrap_execute);
```

is called only after the execution of a statement. If the `kernel_ready` event is executed fast and triggers `request_kernel_list`, this event will be triggered. However, the `kernel_ready` event seems to be triggered rather slowly so if we execute an excel immediately after a notebook is opened, the cell will be executed by the original executor.

I am not sure why `kernel_ready` is triggered so slowly .

issue_title: RQ/Cluster interface
issue_body:
Command

```
sos start server [-e rq|mrq|celery]
sos stop server ID
```
1. start a redis server
2. write .sos/server.yaml

```
sos start worker [-e rq|mrq|celery]
sos stop worker ID
```
1. check `.sos/server.yaml` for redis server
2. wait if redis server is not started (e.g. still in PBS queue)
3. try to connect to redis server and check if it is alive
4. start working once redis server is alive

```
sos start monitor
```
1. check .sos/server.yaml for redis server
2. start a browser and point to rq monitor interface

```
sos run myscript [-e rq|mrq|celery]
```
1. check .sos/server.yaml for redis server
2. connect to redis server
3. submit tasks to redis server

Of course we do not have to support multiple backend but the interface should be general enough if we need to switch.

issue_title: Implement a `no_wait` feature for actions and nested workflows
issue_body:
SoS nested workflow executes sequentially. It makes it hard to parallel scenarios like: 

```
sos_run('A+B+C')
sos_run('A+D+C')
```
where B and D can in principle execute at the same time as long as A completes. Currently the only way to make it happen is to write the script in `Make` style. Here we propose:

```
sos_run('A+B+C', no_wait = True)
sos_run('A+D+C', no_wait = True)
```
and for actions 

```
run: no_wait = True
...

python: no_wait = True
```

For nested workflow this will result in consolidating these sequences to the master DAG before execution. For actions this will result in no_wait actions to be sent to background for execution while the executor takes in the next action in line.

issue_title: learner.fit() duplicate keyword arguments
issue_body:
### Problem

When working through the lang_model.ipynb notebook the following code produced the following error:

```
TEXT = data.Field(lower=True)
FILES = dict(train='wiki.train.tokens', validation='wiki.valid.tokens', test='wiki.test.tokens')
bs,bptt = 80,70
md = LanguageModelData.from_text_files(PATH, TEXT, **FILES, bs=bs, bptt=bptt, min_freq=10)
len(md.trn_dl), md.nt, len(md.trn_ds), len(md.trn_ds[0].text)

em_sz = 200
nh = 500
nl = 3
learner = md.get_model(SGD_Momentum(0.7), em_sz, nh, nl)
reg_fn=partial(seq2seq_reg, alpha=2, beta=1)

clip=0.3
learner.fit(10, 1, wds=1e-6, reg_fn=reg_fn, clip=clip)
```

Error:
```
TypeError: fit() got multiple values for keyword argument 'reg_fn'
```

### Reason For Problem
In `learner.py` in the final call to `fit()` (in `fit_gen()`) both `**kwargs` and `reg_fn` and `clip` get passed to `fit()` which means that if you already specified a `reg_fn` and/or a value for `clip` when calling `learner.fit()` then we end up with duplicate keyword arguments because `reg_fn` and `clip` have already been defined as instance variables of `learner` in the constructor. 

### Proposed Solution
Maybe the API has just evolved so that you always pass `reg_fn` and `clip` to the `learner` constructor. But if the idea is to maintain the ability to pass in different `reg_fn`s and `clip` values at different stages of training then maybe it would be better to check in `fit_gen()` if `reg_fn` or `clip` are already present in the `**kwargs` and if so to pull them out before passing them along to `fit()`.

issue_title: OWL Namespace Definition Support
issue_body:
Background:

- Given `*.owl` file, detect the format (Manchester, OWL/XML, RDF/XML, etc.)
- Converting RDF/XML to OWL/XML
  - Many of the ontologies through the EBI [Ontology Lookup Service](http://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols/index) are serialized in RDF/XML format
  - Manchester [Owl Syntax Converter](http://owl.cs.manchester.ac.uk/tools/webapps/owl-syntax-converter/) implements the Java libaray, [owlapi](https://github.com/owlcs/owlapi). It doesn't look like there's a huge python ecosystem for using OWL.
- The `owlready` module ([PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/Owlready/0.2), [docs](http://pythonhosted.org/Owlready)) can parse OWL/XML format only
- Possible other resources [owllib](https://github.com/joshhanna/owllib), [ontosPy](https://github.com/lambdamusic/ontosPy), RDFLib's [OWL-RL](https://github.com/RDFLib/OWL-RL)
- Load relevant information into `nx.DiGraph` where node names are the names within the namespace, and edges represent `(child, parent)` relationships

Goals:
- [x] Write function `pybel.utils.parse_owl`

```python
def parse_owl(path):
    """Parses an owl document and loads to directed graph

    :param path: file path or URL to OWL file
    :type path: str
    :rtype: networkx.DiGraph
    """
    pass
```

- [x] Add `DEFINE NAMESPACE xxx AS OWL http://example.com` to `MetadataParser`
- [x] Populate namespace with classes, subclasses, and instances from ontology
- [x] Write test BEL file with OWL namespaces and accompanying unit tests

issue_title: race conditions, output widgets, and matplotlib
issue_body:
I ran into some strange behavior when trying to show matplotlib plots when background threads complete.  IIUC, matplotlib maintains global state and doesn't work well with multiple threads, but I'm still seeing some strange behavior even with a lock around code that renders plots to particular output widgets: some plots never show up, even though the IPython kernel seems to be idle.

Also, without locks, the `with output_widget: ...` functionality (which is super cool btw!) doesn't work well: things like plots and printed messages end up all over the place if different threads are trying to produce output.  It'd be nice if users didn't have to put locks in their code.

It's very possible I'm missing something here, but I didn't see any answers in a quick search on GitHub and stackoverflow (a few semi-related issues are linked below).


This is pretty complicated, so here's as minimal an example as I could come up with:

```python
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib
import numpy as np
import ipywidgets
from IPython.display import display
from threading import RLock

print("Widgets: {}".format(ipywidgets.__version__))
print("MPL: {}".format(matplotlib.__version__))

# some data to plot
x = np.arange(1000) + 1
y = np.log(x)

# number of plots to make
n = 10

# make some output widgets.
widgets = [ipywidgets.Output() for _ in range(n)]
# I also noticed that if the output widgets aren't initialized with some text or plot,
# then the plots end up in arbitrary order (e.g., try commenting out the for loop here).
for w in widgets:
    with w:
        print('loading')
display(*widgets)


# a function that takes a little while to run, after which plotting will happen.
def foo():
    for _ in range(1000):
        np.random.rand()

# a lock since matplotlib isn't thread safe.
# without this lock, the plots go all over the place (to the wrong output widgets?)
lock = RLock()

# callback to render each plot when foo is done
def make_render_func(i, widget):
    def render_plot(_):
        with lock:
            widget.clear_output()
            with widget:
                fig, ax = plt.subplots()
                ax.plot(x, y, color='blue')
                ax.set_title("plot %d" % i)
                plt.show()
    return render_plot

# make a future for each plot
ex = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=n)
for i in range(n):
    f = ex.submit(foo)    
    f.add_done_callback(make_render_func(i, widgets[i]))

```

Here's an example of what happens (plot 8 never shows up):

![screen shot 2017-09-20 at 9 33 38 am](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/181744/30649792-d74b9232-9de6-11e7-9238-099e4b1a210a.png)


Possibly related (?):
* https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/1474
* https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/issues/565
* https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/issues/1602

Some version info:

```
jupyter==1.0.0
jupyter-client==5.1.0
jupyter-console==5.2.0
jupyter-core==4.3.0
```

```
 ~ » python --version
Python 3.6.2 :: Continuum Analytics, Inc.
```

Mac OS 10.12.6

Would correctly return:

HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
Server: BaseHTTP/0.3 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 06 Jan 2018 19:25:50 GMT
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: 0
Server: Werkzeug/0.14.1 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 06 Jan 2018 19:25:50 GMT
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: HEAD,GET,PUT,POST,DELETE,OPTIONS,PATCH
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization,content-type,content-md5,x-amz-content-sha256,x-amz-date,x-amz-security-token,x-amz-user-agent
```

Starting with 0.8.4, the same call returns:

```
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content

Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8

issue_title: Argument types with keywords in them fail to be parsed correctly
issue_body:
If a C++ function is defined like:

```cpp
evolve(const structure::QuantumSystem &);
```

then when parsed by Doxylink it will see it as

```cpp
evolve(const ure::QuantumSystem &);
```

since it's matching against `struct` and removing the string from the match.

Instead, the parser should only match those on whitespace boundaries.

issue_title: Why this variable is called `critic_inputs`?
refactor images for Python compatibility
issue_body:
I think the next step in the Python wrapping is to refactor the ```Image``` class so it can share data with NumPy arrays.

Here are the details of what I plan to do:

 - Remove the ```FITSFile``` and ```FITSImage``` classes entirely.

 - Remove the "header" component of ```Image```.

 - Reimplement ```Image``` internals to use ```boost::shared_ptr``` for reference counting; this can use an arbitrary deleter and will allow an ```Image``` to hold memory blocks allocated by NumPy.

 - Re-write existing example code that uses FITS images in Python with PyFITS.

 - Remove the CFITSIO bits from the build system.

I don't think it will be necessary to modify the ```Image``` public interface aside from removing the header access.

I plan to work on this on a branch...I'm tentatively planning to call it "issues/12".  As this is the first feature branch, If you'd prefer descriptively-named branches, please speak up - I don't have a strong preference, but it's something we should decide on.

issue_title: Fully document interface
issue_body:
eik...@gmail.com, 2010-02-13T01:26:48.000Z 
Clean up and fully document interface. Currently the interface and various plugin implementations 
are intermixed; the interface is easier to define and document if we separate one from the other. 
Also, we could benefit from defining __all__ in the interface modules to future clarify what's part of 
the interface vs. an implementation side effect (see the email package in the standard library for an 
example of this).

    def test_generate_key(self):
